proposals.title: Life Years Lost and Cause-Specific Mortality: Refining Risk Estimates for Cancers with Weak Associations in a Large, Longitudinal Cohort
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies often focus on diseases with strong risk associations (e.g., RR>2.0), leaving cancers with modest relative risks (RR<1.20) under-investigated despite their significant population burden. Rectal cancer and leukemia exemplify this gap, where weak but consistent associations with factors like mental illness or socioeconomic position may be obscured by confounding or insufficient statistical power in standard models. Refining these estimates is crucial for accurate public health prioritization and understanding the full mortality impact of complex exposures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying advanced life table and survival analysis methods to a large, longitudinal cohort with detailed individual-level data, we can detect and precisely quantify the mortality burden and life years lost associated with cancers currently characterized by weak risk ratios.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using the comprehensive claims data of a South African medical insurance scheme, extending methods from our prior work on mental illness. For exposures of interest (e.g., prior mental health diagnosis, socioeconomic index), we will calculate cause-specific hazard ratios for rectal cancer and leukemia mortality using multi-state and competing risks survival models. We will refine estimates by stratifying on key covariates (age, sex, comorbidities) and employing parametric survival models (Weibull, Gompertz) to extrapolate survival curves and calculate life years lost (LYL) with greater precision than standard Cox models allow.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, stratified risk estimates and LYL metrics for cancers with weak associations, directly informing targeted screening and prevention strategies. It will demonstrate a methodological framework for extracting precise signals from high-dimensional, real-world data where conventional analyses may be underpowered.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific application of advanced survival and life table methodologies, proven in mental health research, to the underexplored domain of weakly associated cancers, moving beyond simple hazard ratios to quantify the population burden in terms of life years lost.

proposals.title: Life years lost to cancer: A pharmacoepidemiologic study of body mass index and mortality using high-dimensional propensity score adjustment
proposals.motivation: The association between body mass index (BMI) and cancer mortality is well-established but subject to substantial confounding by comorbidities, concurrent medications, and healthcare utilization patterns. Traditional observational studies often inadequately control for these complex confounders, leading to residual bias. Advanced pharmacoepidemiology methods, proven in drug safety research, offer a robust framework to address this in large-scale health data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying high-dimensional propensity score (hdPS) methods to a large medical insurance claims database will substantially reduce confounding in the BMI-cancer mortality association compared to conventional adjustment, revealing a more precise and potentially modified estimate of life years lost.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using data from a South African medical insurance scheme, leveraging Dr. Egger's prior work with this data. The exposure will be time-updated BMI categories. The primary outcome will be cancer-specific mortality and life years lost. We will implement hdPS, empirically identifying hundreds of potential confounders from diagnosis, procedure, and medication codes. The hdPS will be used for matching or weighting, and results will be compared to those from conventional propensity score models and multivariable regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more rigorous, confounder-adjusted estimate of the mortality burden of BMI-related cancers, directly extending Dr. Egger's work on life years lost. It will demonstrate the utility of pharmacoepidemiologic techniques for non-pharmacologic exposures in complex real-world data. The findings will inform targeted cancer prevention strategies by clarifying the independent risk posed by elevated BMI.
proposals.novelty: While hdPS is standard in drug studies, its application to estimate the mortality burden of a physiological metric like BMI in a non-Western, insured population is novel. This bridges Dr. Egger's expertise in life years lost, socioeconomic indices, and cohort studies with cutting-edge methods for causal inference in observational data.

proposals.title: A Design-Based Framework and Checklist for Proactively Mitigating Mortality Bias in Observational Studies of Mental and Physical Health
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread adoption of reporting guidelines (e.g., STROBE) and bias analysis tools, systematic biases, particularly immortal time bias and selection bias related to mortality, persist in observational studies of life years lost and mortality among people with mental and physical health conditions. These biases, often introduced at the design stage, compromise the validity of evidence used for health policy and clinical guidelines. A shift from post-hoc reporting to proactive design intervention is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, design-focused framework and checklist, applied prospectively during study protocol development, will significantly reduce the risk of introducing critical biases (especially immortal time and selection bias) in cohort studies investigating mortality and life years lost.
proposals.method: We will develop the framework through a multi-stage process: 1) A systematic review of pre-2026 literature to catalogue design flaws in studies of mortality among patients with mental illness or conditions like candidaemia. 2) Expert consensus (Delphi method) with epidemiologists, statisticians, and clinical researchers to prioritize critical design decisions. 3) Formalization of a checklist and flow-diagram tool, inspired by CONSORT but for design, guiding choices on cohort entry, exposure definition, and follow-up time to align with target estimands. 4) Pilot validation by re-designing protocols from past studies, including those on COVID-19 and life years lost, to demonstrate bias reduction via simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, actionable tool for researchers to preemptively avoid common biases, moving beyond mere reporting. It will directly enhance the methodological rigor of cohort studies in epidemiology and health services research, leading to more reliable estimates of mortality risk and life years lost for vulnerable populations.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools (e.g., ROBINS-I) assess risk of bias, they are evaluative and applied after the fact. Our framework is generative and prescriptive, intended to be used actively during the study planning phase to engineer robustness into the design itself, which is a fundamental inversion of the current paradigm.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Clinical Trial Design from Incomplete Reports: An Inverse Problem Approach for Mental Health and Mortality Studies
proposals.motivation: Incomplete reporting of clinical trials, particularly in mental health and mortality research, hampers evidence synthesis, reproducibility, and the accurate assessment of treatment effects. This is a critical issue for life expectancy and long-term outcome studies, where complex designs and analyses are often summarized inadequately. A systematic method to reconstruct the original trial parameters from published summaries is urgently needed to improve secondary analyses and meta-research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing incomplete trial reporting as an inverse problem, we can develop computational methods that accurately reconstruct key design elements (e.g., sample size, allocation ratios, follow-up time) and analysis parameters from the statistical summaries typically found in published reports.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, a natural language processing (NLP) module, based on a fine-tuned BERT architecture, will extract structured data (e.g., reported hazard ratios, confidence intervals, p-values, patient counts) from the text and tables of trial publications. Second, an optimization framework will treat the reported statistics as constraints and solve the inverse problem using iterative algorithms (e.g., Monte Carlo sampling with likelihood-based scoring) to infer the most probable underlying trial data and design that could have generated the published results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool for meta-researchers to audit and harmonize evidence from imperfectly reported trials, particularly in fields like psychiatric epidemiology and mortality research. It will enhance the validity of secondary data analyses and systematic reviews by enabling more accurate reconstruction of study parameters for re-analysis or inclusion in individual participant data meta-analyses.
proposals.novelty: While NLP for data extraction exists, applying inverse problem-solving to statistically reconstruct trial designs from sparse published outputs is a novel conceptual shift. This moves beyond information retrieval to the generative modeling of plausible trial datasets consistent with reported findings.

proposals.title: A framework for dynamic, living trial reports: Integrating cumulative mortality and morbidity outcomes in long-term mental health studies
proposals.motivation: Traditional trial reporting is static, creating a lag between evidence generation and dissemination, which is critical for interventions affecting long-term outcomes like mortality in mental health. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for rapid, iterative evidence synthesis. This gap is particularly acute for chronic conditions where outcomes like life-years lost accrue over time, as shown in pre-2026 cohort studies.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, machine-readable framework for dynamic trial reports will improve the timeliness, transparency, and clinical utility of cumulative outcome data (e.g., mortality, hospitalizations) compared to static reporting, without compromising scientific rigor.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the CONSORT reporting guidelines to create a 'Living CONSORT' framework. This involves developing a versioned, structured data schema (using JSON-LD) to encode key time-varying results (e.g., Kaplan-Meier estimates, hazard ratios) and metadata. We will implement a prototype web platform using the R Shiny framework and the 'flexdashboard' package to automatically generate interactive reports from this schema, allowing for incremental updates. The framework's feasibility will be piloted using de-identified data from long-term mental health cohort studies, similar to those in Dr. Egger's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, open-source technical standard and toolset for producing living reports, directly addressing calls for more responsive evidence systems. It will demonstrate how longitudinal health outcomes research can be more dynamically integrated into clinical and policy decision-making cycles.
proposals.novelty: While living systematic reviews exist, the proposed framework focuses on the primary trial report as the dynamic unit, creating a pipeline from data analysis to public dissemination. It uniquely integrates epidemiological cohort study methods with real-time web technologies for reporting.

proposals.title: Development and validation of the Critical Appraisal of Study Quality (CASQ) tool: A machine learning approach to assess methodological rigor in observational health research
proposals.motivation: Existing tools like the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale or ROBINS-I focus on reporting completeness or risk of bias, but do not directly assess the underlying methodological quality of a study's design and execution. This gap is critical in fields like psychiatric epidemiology and life-years-lost research, where strong causal inference depends on methodological rigor, not just complete reporting. A tool that evaluates quality directly would improve evidence synthesis and guideline development.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model, trained on expert judgments and key methodological features extracted from study texts, can reliably and automatically score the intrinsic methodological quality of observational cohort studies, independent of their adherence to reporting checklists.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised machine learning model using a labeled dataset of observational studies from fields like mental health and infectious disease (e.g., candidaemia). Labels (quality scores) will be derived from a Delphi consensus of methodologies. Features will be extracted from full-text PDFs using NLP techniques, focusing on design elements (e.g., cohort assembly, exposure/outcome measurement, confounding control) rather than reporting presence. We will compare algorithm performance (e.g., Random Forest, BERT-based architectures) against expert ratings and existing tools.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a validated, automated tool for the critical appraisal of study quality, moving beyond reporting checklists. It will enable systematic reviewers and guideline panels (like those for COVID-19 or mental health) to more efficiently and objectively prioritize high-quality evidence. The tool will be integrated into platforms like the Meta-Research Innovation Center at Stanford (METRICS) ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing tools that check for the presence of reported items, this tool aims to algorithmically assess the *adequacy* and *rigor* of the methods described. It shifts the focus from 'was it reported?' to 'was it done well?', a fundamental but under-automated distinction in evidence-based medicine.

proposals.title: Identifying high-impact subpopulations for mortality reduction: A PYLL-based analysis of mental and physical health conditions in a Swiss national cohort
proposals.motivation: Traditional mortality analyses often focus on death counts or relative risks, which can obscure the burden borne by younger populations. Potential Years of Life Lost (PYLL) is a crucial metric for public health prioritization, but its application to identify specific subpopulations where therapeutic interventions could yield the greatest life-year gains remains underdeveloped, particularly for mental-physical multimorbidity. This is vital for efficient resource allocation in health systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inverting standard survival analysis to focus on PYLL will identify distinct, younger subpopulations with specific mental-physical health condition profiles (e.g., severe mental illness comorbid with cardiovascular disease) that contribute disproportionately to the total life-years lost, even in eras of effective therapy, and that these profiles differ from those identified by mortality rate alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked Swiss national mortality and health insurance claims data (pre-2026). For individuals who died, we will calculate PYLL using standard life tables. We will employ a decomposition analysis (e.g., Shapley value decomposition) to attribute total PYLL to specific disease combinations, sociodemographic factors (using the Swiss-SEP index), and healthcare utilization patterns. Machine learning techniques, specifically gradient-boosted models (XGBoost), will be used to predict high PYLL risk and identify key interaction features, moving beyond simple regression models for mortality counts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, actionable framework for health policy by pinpointing the specific patient profiles where interventions could save the most life years. It will extend the concept of PYLL from a descriptive population metric to a targeted, patient-level tool for prioritization, directly applicable to integrated care models for mental and physical health.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the analytical perspective from 'risk of dying' to 'impact on life-years lost' and combining PYLL decomposition with machine learning to identify high-impact multimorbidity clusters, a methodologically distinct approach from Dr. Egger's prior work on life years lost and guideline adherence.

proposals.title: Dynamic trajectories of injection drug use and their impact on mortality during antiretroviral therapy: a longitudinal cohort study
proposals.motivation: In HIV research, a history of injection drug use (IDU) is often treated as a fixed baseline risk factor for mortality. This static view fails to capture the dynamic nature of substance use, which can change during the course of therapy. Understanding how transitions in drug use status (e.g., cessation, initiation, relapse) influence survival is critical for developing timely, targeted interventions to improve outcomes in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic changes in IDU status after ART initiation are stronger predictors of mortality than baseline IDU history alone, and that patients who cease IDU after starting therapy will have mortality rates approaching those of patients with no history of IDU.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from established cohorts (e.g., the Swiss HIV Cohort Study, pre-2026 data). IDU status will be treated as a time-updated covariate. We will employ multi-state survival models (e.g., illness-death models) to estimate transition probabilities between states of active IDU, non-active IDU, and death. The primary analysis will compare mortality hazard ratios from Cox models with baseline-only IDU classification versus time-updated classification, adjusting for clinical and socioeconomic confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence for a paradigm shift from viewing IDU as a static trait to a modifiable risk factor during care. It will quantify the survival benefit of reducing or ceasing injection drug use after ART initiation, offering a stronger evidence base for integrating harm reduction and substance use treatment into HIV care pathways.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links baseline IDU to mortality, this study explicitly models IDU as a time-varying exposure using advanced multi-state methods, directly testing the limitation of the fixed-trait assumption prevalent in the literature.

proposals.title: Life years lost associated with mental illness in sub-Saharan Africa: a multi-country cohort study of people living with HIV
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Egger et al. (2021) quantified life years lost due to mental illness in a South African insured cohort, but this population had relatively good healthcare access. In low-income settings, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, the intersection of HIV, mental illness, and constrained health systems creates a critical evidence gap. Understanding mortality and life expectancy in this 'real-world' context is essential for equitable global health policy and integrated service planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the life years lost associated with comorbid mental illness among people living with HIV on antiretroviral therapy (ART) is substantially greater in low-income, public-sector health systems compared to middle-income, insured populations, and that this disparity is mediated by factors like ART adherence, advanced HIV disease at presentation, and socioeconomic deprivation.
proposals.method: We will establish a collaborative cohort analysis using existing longitudinal data from 3-5 public-sector HIV treatment cohorts in East and Southern Africa (e.g., from IeDEA consortium). The primary exposure is a diagnosed mental disorder (e.g., depression, substance use). We will apply parametric survival models (flexible parametric survival models or Royston-Parmar models) to estimate life expectancy and years of life lost (YLL) for those with vs. without mental illness, adjusting for age, sex, CD4 count, ART duration, and a validated area-level socioeconomic index. Mediation analysis will assess pathways via late presentation and adherence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first multi-country estimates of life expectancy loss for people living with HIV and mental illness in low-income settings, directly informing WHO guidelines on integrating mental health into HIV care. It will quantify the mortality burden of this comorbidity in resource-constrained environments and identify key modifiable mediators to target with interventions.
proposals.novelty: While the core method of calculating life years lost is established, its application to this vulnerable population in public-sector African HIV care, and the explicit comparison of the mortality gap between resource settings, is novel. It moves beyond a single insured cohort to a generalizable public health context where the burden is greatest but data are scarce.

proposals.title: Causes and determinants of non-AIDS mortality in an aging Swiss population on long-term antiretroviral therapy: a retrospective cohort study
proposals.motivation: With the success of combination antiretroviral therapy (ART), people with HIV are living longer, shifting the mortality profile from AIDS-defining illnesses to non-AIDS causes. However, the specific leading causes of death (e.g., cardiovascular disease, liver disease, non-AIDS malignancies) and their relative contributions in aging, long-term treated populations in high-income settings like Switzerland are not fully quantified. Understanding this evolving epidemiology is crucial for tailoring preventive care and optimizing long-term health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a cohort of individuals on long-term ART (>10 years), non-AIDS causes, particularly cardiovascular disease and malignancies, will account for the majority of deaths, and that mortality risk will be associated with traditional risk factors, duration of ART, and specific prior antiretroviral drug exposures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using data from the Swiss HIV Cohort Study (SHCS), linking participant records with cause-of-death information from the Swiss National Cohort and mortality statistics. The primary outcome is cause-specific mortality. We will employ competing risks regression (Fine-Gray models) to estimate subdistribution hazard ratios for specific non-AIDS mortality causes, treating AIDS-related mortality as a competing event. Analyses will be adjusted for demographic, clinical (e.g., CD4 count, viral load), lifestyle factors, and time-updated ART exposure, building on established methods from our prior work on life years lost.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed, contemporary profile of non-AIDS mortality in a well-characterized, aging ART population, quantifying the burden of specific chronic diseases. It will identify modifiable risk factors and treatment history elements associated with elevated risk, informing targeted screening and intervention strategies within HIV care. The findings will contribute essential data for health policy and resource planning for the aging HIV-positive population.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe increased non-AIDS mortality, this analysis focuses specifically on a long-term treated cohort with extensive follow-up, uses robust competing risks methodology to avoid overestimation, and integrates detailed, time-updated treatment data to investigate associations with specific ART regimens and duration, moving beyond traditional risk factors alone.

proposals.title: Microsimulation of Life Years Lost and Healthcare Costs Under Alternative ART Initiation Guidelines: A Policy Analysis for South Africa
proposals.motivation: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) initiation guidelines have evolved, but the long-term population-level health and economic impacts of different CD4 threshold policies in high-HIV-prevalence settings like South Africa remain uncertain. This is critical for resource allocation, especially given the competing health priorities and economic constraints highlighted by the COVID-19 pandemic. A robust, forward-looking analysis is needed to inform sustainable HIV policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a microsimulation model, calibrated to South African cohort data, will demonstrate that earlier ART initiation (e.g., at higher CD4 counts) yields greater gains in life expectancy and is cost-saving or cost-effective over a lifetime horizon compared to later initiation, despite higher initial drug costs.
proposals.method: We will adapt a discrete-time, state-transition microsimulation model from health economics, using a first-order Monte Carlo approach. The model will be parameterized using data from the South African medical insurance scheme cohort (as used in Egger's prior work on life years lost), integrating pre-2026 evidence on mortality, ART adherence, disease progression, and healthcare costs. Key algorithms will include competing-risk survival analysis for transition probabilities and probabilistic sensitivity analysis (using bootstrapping or predefined distributions) to quantify uncertainty in outcomes like quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) and total costs under different guideline scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, transparent microsimulation tool for HIV policy evaluation in South Africa. It will deliver concrete estimates of long-term survival benefits and fiscal implications of ART guidelines, directly supporting national and provincial health departments in priority-setting. The work extends Egger's research on mortality and life years lost into a dynamic, economic evaluation framework.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses exist, applying a detailed microsimulation model—common in non-communicable diseases—to ART policy in a high-burden African setting is novel. It moves beyond cohort life-table methods to model individual heterogeneity and competing risks dynamically, offering a more granular tool for policy than static models.

proposals.title: Development and validation of an individual-level predictive model for life expectancy in a cohort with mental illness: a machine learning approach
proposals.motivation: While population-level studies, including Dr. Egger's prior work, have quantified the substantial life years lost associated with mental illness, this evidence does not directly translate to clinical decision-making for individual patients. In the pre-2026 context of personalized medicine, there is a critical need for tools that can provide personalized risk stratification to inform targeted interventions and resource allocation. A predictive model using baseline clinical and socioeconomic characteristics could bridge this gap between epidemiological findings and clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on a comprehensive set of baseline characteristics—including psychiatric diagnosis, physical comorbidities, medication use, and socioeconomic data (e.g., Swiss-SEP index)—can accurately predict individual all-cause mortality risk and estimate personal life expectancy within this high-risk cohort.
proposals.method: We will utilize the existing South African medical insurance cohort data, linking individual-level records on mental health diagnoses, healthcare utilization, demographics, and area-level socioeconomic position to mortality outcomes. We will employ a survival analysis framework using pre-2026 state-of-the-art algorithms, specifically comparing the performance of a Random Survival Forest (RSF) and a Cox model with elastic-net regularization (Cox-EN) for feature selection. Model performance will be evaluated using time-dependent Area Under the Curve (AUC) and calibration plots, with internal validation via bootstrapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinically interpretable tool for estimating personalized mortality risk in individuals with mental illness, moving beyond group averages. It will identify the key modifiable risk factors (e.g., specific comorbidities, treatment gaps) that most strongly drive individual prognosis, providing actionable targets for clinical intervention. The model could serve as a foundational component for future dynamic risk assessment tools.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying advanced survival machine learning techniques to the specific challenge of individual prognosis in mental health, integrating rich clinical and socioeconomic data from a real-world cohort to shift from population-level 'years lost' metrics to personalized predictions, which is a significant and non-trivial translational step.

proposals.title: Ecological analysis of population-level trends in dietary patterns, insulin-like growth factor levels, and cancer incidence in Switzerland, 2000-2025
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence links insulin-like growth factor (IGF) signaling to cancer risk, and modern diets may elevate IGF-1 levels. However, long-term, population-level evidence on how dietary shifts correlate with changes in IGF biomarkers and subsequent cancer incidence is sparse. Ecological studies can provide crucial macro-level insights to generate hypotheses for individual-level research and inform public health strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, at the population level, temporal increases in the consumption of animal proteins and ultra-processed foods are positively correlated with population mean IGF-1 levels, which in turn are positively correlated with the incidence of IGF-sensitive cancers (e.g., colorectal, prostate, premenopausal breast) over a 25-year period in Switzerland.
proposals.method: We will employ an ecological time-trend study design using aggregated Swiss national data. Annual data on food availability (from Swiss Federal Office for Agriculture), mean IGF-1 levels (from pooled Swiss cohort studies and health surveys), and age-standardized cancer incidence rates (from the National Institute for Cancer Epidemiology and Registration, NICER) will be compiled for 2000-2025. We will use joinpoint regression to identify significant trend changes and time-lagged generalized estimating equations (GEE) to model correlations between dietary factors, IGF levels (with appropriate lag periods), and subsequent cancer incidence, adjusting for confounders like aging and obesity prevalence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, macro-level perspective on the long-term interplay between diet, a key biological pathway (IGF), and cancer burden in a high-income country. It will quantify population-attributable trends, offering evidence to prioritize nutritional public health interventions. The methodology will demonstrate the utility of ecological designs for hypothesis generation in nutritional epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While individual-level diet-IGF-cancer links are studied, a longitudinal ecological analysis spanning 25 years at a national level is novel. It integrates disparate national data streams (diet supply, biomarker pools, cancer registries) to model time-lagged population effects, a method not commonly applied to this specific hypothesis in the Swiss context.

proposals.title: Empirical ranking of STROBE checklist items by their association with study interpretability and bias in observational epidemiology
proposals.motivation: The STROBE statement is a critical tool for improving reporting in observational studies, but its 22-item checklist treats all components as equally important. This assumption lacks empirical validation and may dilute efforts to improve the most consequential reporting flaws. Given the surge in observational research on topics like COVID-19 and mental health mortality, a data-driven hierarchy is needed to guide authors, peer reviewers, and journal editors toward the most impactful reporting improvements.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific STROBE items, particularly those concerning confounding, selection bias, and missing data, have a stronger empirical association with metrics of study interpretability and risk of bias than items about title wording or funding sources.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-epidemiological study. A systematic sample of 300 observational cohort studies published 2015-2023 on mortality (e.g., after COVID-19, or among people with mental illness) will be identified. Two independent raters will score each study's adherence to each STROBE item and assess its overall interpretability and risk of bias using a modified ROBINS-I tool. We will use random forest regression and Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to model the non-linear relationships and rank the relative importance of each STROBE item in predicting low bias/high interpretability scores. Sensitivity analyses will adjust for journal impact factor and study size.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first empirically derived, prioritized STROBE checklist, moving reporting guidance from a one-size-fits-all to a risk-based approach. It will provide concrete evidence to streamline manuscript and peer review checklists, focusing effort on the reporting gaps that most threaten validity, especially in life-course and clinical epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have assessed STROBE adherence, none have used machine learning explainability techniques to empirically rank items by their actual impact on study quality, moving beyond expert consensus to data-driven hierarchy.

proposals.title: STROBE-FLOW: A standardized framework for participant flow diagrams in observational cohort and case-control studies
proposals.motivation: The CONSORT flow diagram is a cornerstone of transparent reporting in randomized trials, but no universally adopted equivalent exists for observational studies. This gap hinders the assessment of selection bias, attrition, and the reproducibility of studies like large-scale cohort analyses of mortality after COVID-19 or life years lost associated with mental illness. A standardized diagram is urgently needed to improve the clarity, comparability, and critical appraisal of observational research, which forms the bulk of epidemiological evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that developing and validating a structured flow diagram template, aligned with the STROBE statement, will significantly improve the completeness and clarity of participant flow reporting in published observational studies compared to current ad-hoc practices.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review of flow diagrams in high-impact cohort and case-control studies (2015-2025) to document current practices and deficiencies. Using a Delphi consensus process with an international panel of epidemiologists, methodologies, and journal editors, we will iteratively develop a mandatory core set of diagram elements (e.g., source population, eligibility criteria, matching, exclusions, analysis sets). The framework will be technically implemented as a downloadable template for major diagramming software and a web-based tool, with specific algorithms to guide branching logic for complex designs like nested case-control studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a freely available, consensus-based reporting tool that directly extends the STROBE guideline. It will enhance the methodological rigor of fields central to Dr. Egger's work, such as life course epidemiology and mental health outcomes research, by making selection processes and analytic populations explicit. Widespread adoption would set a new standard for transparency in observational research.
proposals.novelty: While extensions to CONSORT exist for other designs, creating a formal, consensus-driven flow diagram specifically for the complex entry points and follow-up pathways of observational studies is novel. It moves beyond general STROBE text recommendations to provide a practical, visual standardization tool.

proposals.title: STROBE-M: Development and validation of a reporting guideline for observational studies using mega-cohorts and biobanks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of mega-cohorts and biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank, All of Us) has enabled powerful longitudinal studies on topics like life-years lost and mortality associated with mental illness. However, the STROBE guideline, designed for single-study analyses, is inadequate for reporting studies from these multi-use, complex datasets, leading to reproducibility crises and hindering meta-research. A tailored guideline is urgently needed to ensure transparent, complete, and consistent reporting of methods and limitations specific to these resources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a STROBE extension for mega-cohorts (STROBE-M) will significantly improve the completeness and transparency of reporting in observational studies derived from such datasets, as measured by a pre-post assessment of published articles.
proposals.method: Following the EQUATOR Network methodology, we will conduct a three-phase Delphi survey with an international panel of epidemiologists, biostatisticians, data scientists, journal editors, and mega-cohort data stewards (n≈30) to identify essential reporting items. Candidate items will address data provenance (versioning, linkage), complex sampling/selection, handling of multi-modal data (genetic, imaging, EHR), and analytic challenges like competing risks and immortal time bias. The final checklist will be validated by comparing the reporting completeness of a sample of pre- and post-guideline publications using the mega-cohorts identified in Egger's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver a consensus-based, field-tested reporting guideline (STROBE-M) to enhance the rigor, reproducibility, and utility of research from large-scale longitudinal datasets. It will directly support meta-research on life-course health and mortality by enabling reliable evidence synthesis and critical appraisal.
proposals.novelty: While STROBE extensions exist for specific designs (e.g., genetic association studies), none address the unique reporting challenges posed by the scale, complexity, and multi-purpose, evolving nature of contemporary mega-cohorts and biobanks, which are central to modern life-course epidemiology.

proposals.title: Evaluating the application of CONSORT guidelines by peer reviewers: an observational study of clinical trial manuscript assessments
proposals.motivation: The CONSORT statement is a critical tool for ensuring transparent and complete reporting of randomized trials, directly impacting the reliability of evidence used in clinical practice and policy. However, its effectiveness hinges on consistent enforcement during peer review. Prior to 2026, studies have assessed reporting quality, but there is limited empirical evidence on whether journal reviewers systematically identify and challenge CONSORT non-adherence, creating a potential gap in the quality control pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peer reviewers for major medical journals inconsistently apply CONSORT criteria, frequently failing to identify specific reporting deficiencies in submitted trial manuscripts, and that this inconsistency is associated with reviewer characteristics and journal policies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective observational study using a dataset of de-identified peer review reports for submitted clinical trial manuscripts (2018-2023) from collaborating journals. We will employ a two-step analysis: 1) Natural Language Processing (NLP) using a pre-trained BERT model fine-tuned to classify text segments from reviews as referencing specific CONSORT checklist items. 2) Multilevel logistic regression to model the probability of a CONSORT item being mentioned, with fixed effects for manuscript quality (pre-scored for CONSORT adherence), reviewer expertise, and journal endorsement level of CONSORT, and random effects for reviewer and manuscript.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative evidence on the real-world application of CONSORT by reviewers, identifying systemic weaknesses in the review process. The findings will inform the development of targeted interventions, such as enhanced reviewer checklists or mandatory reporting forms for journals, to strengthen peer review and ultimately improve the quality of published trial evidence.
proposals.novelty: While previous research has analyzed published papers for CONSORT adherence, this study innovatively shifts the focus to the peer review process itself, leveraging a novel dataset of review reports and NLP methods to objectively measure reviewer behavior, a previously unobserved component of the publication ecosystem.

proposals.title: The Programmatic Reporting Checklist (PRC): A framework for transparent reporting of linked clinical trial programs in mental health and beyond
proposals.motivation: The reporting of individual clinical trials has been standardized by guidelines like CONSORT, but research programs consisting of multiple, methodologically linked trials (e.g., platform, basket, or sequential trials) lack a unified reporting framework. This gap hinders the assessment of program-wide validity, the synthesis of evidence across related studies, and the understanding of cumulative impact on patient outcomes, such as mortality and life years lost, which are central to Matthias Egger's research themes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, consensus-based reporting checklist for entire clinical trial programs will significantly improve the transparency, reproducibility, and interpretability of complex, multi-trial research, leading to better evidence synthesis and policy impact.
proposals.method: The project will adapt the Delphi consensus methodology, engaging an international panel of trialists, methodologies, statisticians, journal editors, and patient representatives over three rounds. The initial checklist items will be derived from a systematic review of existing reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT extensions) and an analysis of reporting deficiencies in published trial programs, particularly in mental health and infectious diseases (leveraging Egger's expertise in cohort studies like ECMM Candida). The final framework will be validated by applying it retrospectively to a sample of trial programs in mental health research.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formal, consensus-based reporting checklist for clinical trial programs, moving beyond single-trial reporting. It will directly support funders, regulators, and researchers in ensuring comprehensive transparency. The framework will be piloted in mental health, a field with high public health burden and complex intervention strategies, with clear applicability to other domains like infectious disease and chronic illness.
proposals.novelty: While checklists for single trials exist, no framework addresses the unique reporting needs of an integrated research program, including the rationale for program structure, cross-trial adaptations, shared control groups, and the synthesis of program-level results and harms.

proposals.title: A Framework for Formal Causal Diagrams in Mendelian Randomization: Application to Life Years Lost Associated with Mental Illness
proposals.motivation: Mendelian Randomization (MR) studies, which are crucial for inferring causality from observational data, often lack rigorous reporting of underlying causal assumptions. This leads to ambiguous interpretations, particularly regarding pleiotropy and confounding pathways. Formalizing these assumptions using Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), a standard in epidemiology, is needed to improve transparency and validity in MR, especially for complex exposures like mental illness linked to mortality.
proposals.hypothesis: The systematic application of a standardized DAG framework to MR analyses will clarify causal assumptions, identify potential biases (e.g., horizontal pleiotropy), and lead to more robust and interpretable estimates of the causal effect of mental illness on life years lost.
proposals.method: We will develop a step-by-step framework for constructing DAGs specific to MR, detailing assumptions about the genetic instrument, exposure, outcome, and confounders. This framework will be applied to a re-analysis of a South African medical insurance cohort study on life years lost and mental illness. We will use established MR algorithms (e.g., inverse-variance weighted, MR-Egger, MR-PRESSO) within the context of the formal DAGs to test for and adjust for identified biases, such as violations of the exclusion restriction criterion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, standardized tool for planning and reporting MR studies, enhancing methodological rigor. It will yield a concrete case study demonstrating how DAGs can improve the causal interpretation of the relationship between mental health and mortality, a key research theme for the investigator.
proposals.novelty: While DAGs are common in epidemiology, their formal and systematic integration into the reporting and analytical workflow of MR is not standardized. This proposal bridges a gap between general epidemiological practice and specific genetic epidemiology methods to address a core weakness in MR.

proposals.title: The association between adherence to the STROBE-MR reporting guideline and subsequent citation impact of Mendelian randomization studies: a systematic review and meta-regression analysis
proposals.motivation: Mendelian randomization (MR) studies, which assess causal effects of exposures on health outcomes, are highly influential in epidemiology but are susceptible to biases if poorly reported. The STROBE-MR guideline was developed to improve reporting transparency. However, it remains unknown whether better reporting, as a marker of methodological rigor and clarity, translates into greater scientific influence, measured by citation rates. This is crucial to incentivize high-quality reporting practices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher adherence to the STROBE-MR reporting guideline in published MR studies is positively associated with their medium-term (2-5 year) citation count, independent of journal impact factor and study topic.
proposals.method: We will systematically identify MR studies published in 2018-2020 from major epidemiological and genetic journals. Two independent reviewers will score each paper using a pre-defined, piloted checklist based on the 20-item STROBE-MR guideline. Citation counts will be extracted from Web of Science/Scopus for a fixed 3-year post-publication window. We will use multivariable negative binomial regression to model citation count, with the STROBE-MR adherence score as the primary exposure, adjusting for confounders (journal impact factor, publication year, field, study design complexity, and author prominence via H-index). Sensitivity analyses will explore non-linear relationships and the influence of specific guideline domains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence on whether adherence to a domain-specific reporting guideline is linked to a tangible academic reward (citations). It will inform guidelines developers, journal editors, and researchers about the potential 'impact dividend' of rigorous reporting. The findings could strengthen the case for mandating guideline adherence during manuscript submission.
proposals.novelty: While the association between general study quality and citations has been explored, this is the first study to specifically and quantitatively link adherence to the contemporary STROBE-MR guideline—a critical tool for causal inference studies—with citation metrics, leveraging Dr. Egger's expertise in life-years, mortality, and cohort methodology.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Trial Quality on Mortality Effect Estimates: A Meta-Epidemiological Study of Mental and Physical Health Interventions
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses often treat all included RCTs as equally reliable, potentially biasing pooled effect estimates. This is critical in fields like mental health and chronic disease management, where trial quality, especially blinding and outcome measurement, is highly variable. A robust, quantitative framework for weighting studies by quality is needed to produce more reliable evidence for clinical guidelines and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that weighting RCTs by a composite metric of quality markers—specifically blinding integrity and objectivity of outcome measurement—will significantly alter pooled mortality effect estimates compared to conventional inverse-variance weighting, with the magnitude of adjustment being greater in mental health than in physical health interventions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-epidemiological study. First, we will systematically sample meta-analyses from pre-2026 literature on interventions for mental health conditions (e.g., depression) and physical health conditions (e.g., cardiovascular disease) where mortality is an outcome. For each primary RCT, we will extract detailed quality data using an augmented Cochrane Risk of Bias 2.0 tool, focusing on blinding of participants/personnel and outcome assessors, and the objectivity of the mortality measure (e.g., all-cause vs. disease-specific). We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical model where the weight of each study in the meta-analysis is a function of both its precision (sample size/variance) and its quality score, modeled as a random effect to estimate the aggregate bias introduced by lower-quality studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, quantitative method for integrating trial quality directly into meta-analytic weights, moving beyond sensitivity analyses. It will produce empirical estimates of how quality distortions vary across medical fields, directly informing GRADE assessments. The findings will enhance the rigor of evidence synthesis in Dr. Egger's areas of expertise, including mortality and mental health.
proposals.novelty: While quality assessment is standard, directly integrating a multi-dimensional quality metric into the weighting algorithm of a meta-analysis is a methodological advance beyond current practice. Applying this specifically to compare its impact on mortality estimates in mental versus physical health interventions addresses a nuanced and underexplored question in evidence hierarchy.

proposals.title: A multi-criteria decision analysis framework for evaluating treatment strategies for severe mental illness: integrating mortality, healthcare costs, and patient-reported outcomes
proposals.motivation: Evaluating treatments for severe mental illness (SMI) requires balancing complex, competing outcomes, including mortality risk, side effects, quality of life, and healthcare costs. Traditional cost-effectiveness analyses often fail to formally integrate patient and clinician preferences for these disparate outcomes. This gap is critical, as suboptimal treatment choices contribute to the significant life years lost among people with SMI, a key finding from Dr. Egger's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a formal Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework to real-world data on SMI will produce a preference-weighted composite outcome that better aligns with stakeholder values and identifies optimal treatment strategies compared to single-outcome or standard cost-effectiveness analyses.
proposals.method: We will apply the EVIDEM (Evidence and Value: Impact on Decision Making) MCDA framework, a well-established pre-2026 methodology. Using data from a Swiss health insurance cohort (similar to prior work), we will define criteria (e.g., mortality reduction, side-effect severity, cost, functional improvement). Weights for these criteria will be derived via discrete choice experiments with psychiatrists, patients, and payers. Treatment strategies (e.g., specific pharmacotherapies, integrated care models) will be scored against each criterion using cohort data and meta-analyses, and aggregated into a weighted value score using a linear additive model for comparative ranking.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a transparent, structured tool for evaluating SMI treatments that incorporates multi-stakeholder preferences. It will extend the life-years-lost paradigm by integrating non-mortality outcomes into a unified metric, offering a more holistic assessment for clinical and health policy decision-making. The framework can be adapted for post-pandemic mental health service evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While MCDA has been used in health technology assessment for somatic diseases, its application to longitudinal, real-world mental health data to create composite outcome measures is novel. It moves beyond reporting separate outcomes (mortality, cost) to formally synthesizing them based on elicited preferences.

proposals.title: Identifying Keystone Interventions and Critical Evidence Gaps in Pharmacotherapy: An Ecological Network Analysis of Comparative Effectiveness Networks
proposals.motivation: Network meta-analyses (NMAs) synthesize evidence from trials comparing multiple treatments, but their structure is rarely analyzed beyond statistical connectivity. This limits the identification of pivotal 'keystone' comparator drugs that anchor the evidence base or critical gaps where direct comparisons are missing. Applying ecological food web metrics offers a novel, systematic way to characterize and optimize these evidence networks for more robust clinical guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metrics from ecological network analysis (e.g., betweenness centrality, trophic level, connectance) can successfully identify keystone comparator drugs within pharmacotherapy networks and quantify the fragility and coherence of the overall evidence structure for clinical decision-making.
proposals.method: We will construct evidence networks from published NMAs in major therapeutic areas (e.g., antidepressants, antihypertensives). Nodes will represent drugs, and links will represent the existence of direct comparative evidence. We will calculate food web metrics using established algorithms (e.g., for centrality, modularity) via the R package 'igraph'. The structural role of drugs (e.g., keystone, peripheral) will be mapped, and network robustness will be tested by simulating the removal of highly central nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel quantitative framework to audit and visualize the architecture of comparative evidence. It will offer systematic methods for guideline developers to prioritize future trials targeting critical evidence gaps and for clinicians to understand the evidential leverage of key comparator drugs used in practice.
proposals.novelty: While ecological network analysis is mature in ecology, its application to evidence synthesis is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive network diagrams to a formal, metric-driven assessment of evidence ecosystem health and vulnerability, a conceptual leap not yet realized in meta-research.

proposals.title: Beyond Reporting Bias: A Quantitative Assessment of Design and Conduct Flaws as Primary Barriers to Validity in Observational Studies of Mortality and Mental Health
proposals.motivation: The dominant focus in research methodology has been on improving reporting standards (e.g., via STROBE guidelines) to combat bias. However, it remains unclear whether inadequate reporting is the primary problem, or if fundamental flaws in study design and conduct are the main barrier to valid causal inference, particularly in complex fields like mental health and mortality where confounding is severe. This distinction is critical for directing methodological efforts and resources effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in observational studies linking mental illness to mortality, a substantial proportion of studies deemed 'unassessable' or at high risk of bias will have irremediable flaws rooted in design and conduct (e.g., inappropriate cohort definition, unmeasured confounding, selection bias), which cannot be resolved by perfect reporting alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and quantitative bias analysis of observational studies (2015-2025) examining mortality or life-years lost in people with mental disorders. Using a structured, piloted tool, two independent reviewers will extract data on: 1) adherence to STROBE reporting items, and 2) the presence and severity of pre-specified design/conduct flaws (e.g., confounding control methods, selection processes, exposure/outcome measurement). We will use latent class analysis to identify clusters of studies with similar flaw profiles and multinomial regression to determine if reporting quality predicts membership in a 'flawed design' cluster, controlling for study characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical quantification of the relative contribution of design/conduct flaws versus reporting gaps to overall study validity. It will challenge the field to move beyond a reporting-centric view of bias and inform the development of more targeted study planning tools and peer-review checklists that prioritize robust design from the outset.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools assess risk of bias, they often conflate reporting with design. This study explicitly disentangles them, testing the non-obvious premise that perfect reporting may not salvage studies with foundational design flaws, a critical but under-researched distinction.

proposals.title: A Real-Time Design Assistant for Observational Cohort Studies: Integrating Reporting Guidelines into the Study Planning Workflow
proposals.motivation: Reporting guidelines like STROBE are used post-hoc to improve manuscript quality, but major methodological flaws, such as inadequate confounding control or outcome definition, are often locked in during the design phase. This is particularly critical in life-course and mental health epidemiology, where Egger's work on mortality and life years lost highlights the impact of study design on valid effect estimation. Proactive guidance could prevent bias before data collection begins.
proposals.hypothesis: That embedding reporting guideline logic into an interactive, real-time design tool will significantly reduce protocol-level deviations from methodological best practices in observational studies, compared to standard ad-hoc design processes.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based application using a rule-based expert system architecture. The core engine will encode the logic of STROBE and RECORD guidelines as machine-actionable checklists. The tool will guide users through a structured protocol design via a dynamic form, prompting for essential elements (e.g., defining exposure, outcome, covariates, data sources) and providing instant feedback using decision trees. For example, when defining a cohort from electronic health records, it will automatically prompt for assessment of data linkage completeness and code validation, directly addressing common pitfalls in studies of COVID-19, mental health, and mortality.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first operational tool that shifts guideline use from a reporting checklist to a design framework. It will contribute a validated, open-source platform for improving the methodological rigor of cohort studies in epidemiology and health services research, directly supporting the design of more reliable studies on patient outcomes and life years lost.
proposals.novelty: While checklists exist, a proactive, interactive system that intervenes at the point of study conception—translating guideline text into structured design decisions—is a novel application of knowledge engineering to epidemiological methodology, moving beyond post-hoc auditing.

proposals.title: A Standardized, Machine-Readable Reporting Framework for Epidemiological Cohort Studies: Application to Mortality and Life Years Lost in Mental Illness
proposals.motivation: Large-scale meta-analyses and pooled data analyses, crucial for robust public health evidence, are hindered by the human-centric, narrative format of most published cohort studies. Manual data extraction is time-consuming, error-prone, and limits the scale and reproducibility of evidence synthesis, particularly for complex outcomes like mortality and life years lost. A shift towards machine-readable reporting standards is needed to accelerate and improve the quality of evidence generation in epidemiology.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, machine-readable reporting standard for key epidemiological parameters (e.g., cohort definitions, follow-up time, mortality rates, life years lost, confounder adjustments) will significantly increase the efficiency, accuracy, and scope of automated data extraction for meta-analysis compared to manual extraction from traditional narrative reports.
proposals.method: We will develop a JSON schema defining mandatory and optional data fields for cohort mortality studies, based on STROBE guidelines and Dr. Egger's prior work. The schema will capture granular data on population, exposure (e.g., mental illness diagnosis), outcomes (death, life years lost), covariates, and statistical models. We will create a proof-of-concept extraction pipeline using a rule-based NLP parser (e.g., spaCy patterns) and a large language model (e.g., GPT-3.5/4 API, pre-2026) fine-tuned on annotated text from existing papers (e.g., from Dr. Egger's previous studies) to populate the schema from PDFs. Performance will be validated against a manually curated gold-standard dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, open-source reporting standard and software tool to automate data extraction from cohort studies. It will directly enhance the feasibility of large, detailed meta-analyses on mortality burdens, such as for mental illnesses. The framework will serve as a model for machine-first scientific reporting, improving transparency and reproducibility in epidemiological research.
proposals.novelty: While reporting guidelines like STROBE exist for human authors and readers, this proposal specifically engineers the output format for computational agents. It moves beyond recommending what to report to defining *how* to structure data algorithmically, bridging the gap between narrative publication and automated evidence synthesis.

proposals.title: Development and validation of a minimal reporting standard for individual survival analyses: the Analysis Reporting Checklist (ARC)
proposals.motivation: Existing reporting guidelines (e.g., STROBE) operate at the study level, but reproducibility crises often stem from insufficient detail in single analyses, such as life-years-lost calculations or mortality risk adjustments. This gap is critical in mental health and cohort studies, where complex modeling choices significantly impact conclusions about mortality and life expectancy. Pre-2026, there is no standardized framework to ensure the complete and transparent reporting of a single analytical procedure within a larger paper.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, minimal reporting checklist for individual survival and epidemiological analyses will significantly improve the completeness of methodological reporting and facilitate the independent re-evaluation of key results in published cohort studies.
proposals.method: We will develop the Analysis Reporting Checklist (ARC) through a modified Delphi process with experts in epidemiology, biostatistics, and mental health research, informed by Egger's prior work on life-years-lost and guideline adherence. The ARC will be structured around core analysis types (e.g., Cox models, life tables) and will mandate reporting of data inputs, model specifications, software/code identifiers, and handling of covariates and missing data. We will validate the ARC by applying it to a random sample of 100 pre-2026 publications on mental illness and mortality, comparing reporting completeness before and after hypothetical ARC application.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, micro-level reporting tool that complements existing guidelines, directly addressing transparency gaps in individual figures and results. It will enhance the reproducibility and critical appraisal of findings in mental health epidemiology and life-course research, fields central to Egger's portfolio.
proposals.novelty: While meta-guidelines exist, the novel concept is a granular, analysis-specific standard that moves beyond study-level reporting to target the fundamental unit of scientific evidence: the single reported result or figure.

proposals.title: The LIFE-REP Framework: A Linked, Interoperable Platform for Enhancing Reproducibility in Longitudinal Health Studies
proposals.motivation: Current epidemiological research, especially on long-term outcomes like mortality and life years lost, suffers from fragmented workflows where study registration, data, code, and reporting are siloed. This fragmentation, evident in large cohort studies like those on mental illness or candidaemia, hinders transparency, reproducibility, and the efficient synthesis of evidence. An integrated system is urgently needed to strengthen the validity of population health findings.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a platform that formally links study protocols (e.g., from registries like ClinicalTrials.gov) to analysis code (e.g., R/Python scripts) and resultant reports (e.g., manuscripts) will significantly improve the auditability, reproducibility, and timeliness of cohort study results compared to conventional disconnected practices.
proposals.method: We will design and prototype a web-based platform using a microservices architecture. Core components will include: 1) a metadata harvester using APIs (e.g., from ClinicalTrials.gov and Open Science Framework) to ingest protocol identifiers and links; 2) a version-controlled code repository (Git-integrated) with containerization (Docker) to capture computational environments; 3) a dynamic document generator (R Markdown/Quarto) that programmatically links results to specific code chunks and source data variables; and 4) a graph database (Neo4j) to model and query the relationships between these entities (protocols, datasets, code versions, results).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a functional prototype of an integrated research reporting ecosystem, specifically tailored for complex cohort analyses. It will provide a proof-of-concept demonstrating reduced manual linkage errors and enhanced traceability from published findings back to original protocols and data, thereby directly addressing reproducibility crises in life-course and clinical epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While tools for individual components (registries, Git, dynamic documents) exist, the novelty lies in their deliberate, automated integration into a single queryable ecosystem for epidemiological research, explicitly modeling the provenance chain as a network, rather than relying on ad-hoc, manual connections.

proposals.title: Electronic Patient-Reported Outcome Monitoring to Reduce Treatment Toxicity and Improve Quality of Life in Patients Receiving FLOT Chemotherapy: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The FLOT regimen (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, docetaxel) is a standard perioperative treatment for locally advanced gastric and gastroesophageal junction cancer, but it is associated with significant toxicity that impairs quality of life and can lead to treatment delays, dose reductions, and increased mortality. Pre-2026 evidence shows that systematic electronic patient-reported outcome (ePRO) monitoring improves symptom control and survival in other cancers (e.g., lung), but its specific impact on managing FLOT's complex toxicity profile remains unquantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the implementation of a systematic ePRO monitoring system for patients undergoing FLOT chemotherapy will lead to a clinically significant reduction in severe treatment-related toxicity (Grade ≥3) and an improvement in patient-reported quality of life, compared to standard clinical monitoring alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of patients initiating FLOT at Swiss tertiary centers. Patients will use a validated ePRO platform (e.g., based on the EORTC QLQ-C30 and STO22 modules) to report symptoms weekly. A rules-based algorithm will trigger automated alerts to the clinical team for predefined severe or worsening symptoms (e.g., febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea). The primary outcome is the incidence of Grade ≥3 adverse events (CTCAE v5.0). Secondary outcomes include quality-of-life trajectories, treatment adherence, unplanned hospitalizations, and survival. We will use multivariable regression and propensity score matching to compare outcomes with a historical control cohort managed with standard care.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the efficacy of ePROs for toxicity management in a high-burden gastrointestinal cancer regimen. It will quantify the potential benefits in terms of clinical outcomes, patient well-being, and healthcare utilization, offering a scalable model for integrating patient-centered data into routine oncology practice to mitigate the life-years lost associated with treatment morbidity.
proposals.novelty: While ePRO studies exist in oncology, this proposal uniquely targets the specific, high-toxicity FLOT regimen and integrates the alert system directly into Swiss clinical workflows, leveraging Dr. Egger's expertise in life-years-lost methodology and large-scale cohort studies to assess impact on both morbidity and mortality.

proposals.title: Testing and Adjusting for Meta-Analytic Dependence: A Spatial-Statistics Inspired Framework for Valid Publication Bias Assessment
proposals.motivation: Publication bias tests, such as Egger's regression, assume statistical independence of study estimates. This assumption is frequently violated in modern meta-analyses due to overlapping authorship, shared data sources, or similar methodologies, leading to inflated Type I error rates and unreliable conclusions about bias. A formal diagnostic for this dependence, analogous to tests for spatial autocorrelation, is critically needed to ensure the validity of bias assessments in fields like mental health and mortality research where meta-analyses are pivotal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a proximity matrix quantifying study overlap (e.g., in authors, institutions, or patient cohorts) can be used to detect significant dependency among effect sizes, and that incorporating this dependency structure into bias-correction models will yield more accurate and statistically valid inferences compared to standard methods that assume independence.
proposals.method: We will adapt Moran's I statistic, defining a connectivity matrix where weights reflect study 'proximity' based on shared authors (from bibliometric data), institutional overlap, and methodological similarity (e.g., study design, measurement tools). This diagnostic will be applied to a corpus of published meta-analyses in mental health and mortality. For meta-analyses showing significant dependency, we will develop and implement a covariance-adjusted version of Egger's regression test, integrating the estimated dependency matrix into a generalized least squares framework, and extend this adjustment to selection models for bias correction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal diagnostic test for dependency in the context of publication bias assessment. It will deliver a practical adjustment method, implemented in open-source software (e.g., R package), allowing meta-analysts to validate the independence assumption and produce bias-corrected estimates with valid confidence intervals even when studies are correlated.
proposals.novelty: While spatial autocorrelation is a classic problem, its direct analogy to meta-analytic dependency has not been operationalized. The novelty lies in formally defining 'meta-analytic proximity' and translating the full spatial diagnostics-and-adjustment workflow to solve a core inferential problem in evidence synthesis.

proposals.title: Assessing and Adjusting for Publication Bias in Meta-Analyses with Correlated or Overlapping Estimates: A Statistical Framework and Application to Mental Health and Mortality Studies
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses in epidemiology and health services research, such as those investigating mortality among people with mental illness, often synthesize estimates from studies that are not independent. This occurs when studies share data sources (e.g., the same national registry), have overlapping cohorts, or report multiple correlated outcomes (e.g., mortality from different causes). Standard tests for publication bias, like Egger's test, assume independence, and their performance is compromised under these conditions, leading to potentially biased conclusions about the evidence base.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the covariance structure between correlated or overlapping study estimates, we can develop more robust statistical tests and adjustment methods for publication bias that outperform existing methods which assume independence.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage methodological framework. First, we will extend the multivariate meta-analysis model to incorporate a covariance matrix for study estimates, derived from known sample overlap or estimated from shared control groups. Second, we will adapt regression-based tests (e.g., an extension of Egger's test) and selection models (e.g., the Copas model) to this correlated structure. The methods will be validated via simulation studies across realistic correlation scenarios and applied to a meta-analysis of life years lost associated with mental health conditions, where studies frequently draw on overlapping national insurance or registry data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with practical, open-source tools (R package) to diagnose and adjust for publication bias in syntheses of complex, non-independent evidence. It will improve the validity of pooled estimates in fields like psychiatric epidemiology and life-course research, where data dependencies are common but often ignored.
proposals.novelty: While methods exist for handling dependent effects in meta-analysis, their integration into formal tests for publication bias is novel. This directly addresses a known but unresolved flaw in applying standard bias assessments to contemporary, interconnected research landscapes.

proposals.title: A CLs-inspired meta-analytic framework for robust evaluation of diagnostic test accuracy in the presence of numerous small, heterogeneous studies
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of small, underpowered studies evaluating diagnostic tests, such as rapid antigen tests for COVID-19 or novel biomarkers for mental health conditions, creates a critical methodological gap. Traditional meta-analytic methods like bivariate models can be unstable or biased when applied to datasets dominated by studies with few participants and zero-cell events, hindering reliable estimation of pooled sensitivity and specificity. This problem mirrors the challenge in particle physics of combining many independent experimental channels, each with minimal observed signal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the CLs (Confidence Level) method and Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE) principles from high-energy physics can provide a statistically rigorous framework for meta-analysis of diagnostic tests, yielding more robust pooled estimates and calibrated measures of evidence strength in small-study settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where each primary study's observed true/false positives and negatives are treated as a low-information channel. The model will explicitly incorporate study-specific risk-of-bias indicators (e.g., from QUADAS-2) as systematic uncertainty 'nuisance parameters,' analogous to physics detector calibrations. The core inference will adapt the CLs method's profile likelihood ratio test to construct a combined confidence region for the test's true sensitivity and specificity, formally accounting for the composite null hypothesis of inadequate diagnostic performance. Estimation will utilize BLUE principles for optimally weighting the small-study data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide epidemiologists and guideline developers with a validated statistical tool for synthesizing evidence from fragmented, small-scale test evaluations, a common scenario in emerging diseases or niche clinical settings. It will advance methodological research by creating a formal bridge between advanced statistical combination techniques in physics and evidence synthesis in medicine.
proposals.novelty: While random-effects models are standard in meta-analysis, the explicit translation of the CLs method and the treatment of study biases as systematic uncertainties within a combined likelihood framework is a novel, non-obvious import from particle physics to diagnostic research.

proposals.title: Estimating True Effect Sizes in Psychiatric Epidemiology: A Bayesian State-Space Model to Correct for Publication Bias in Meta-Analyses of Mortality and Life Years Lost
proposals.motivation: Publication bias systematically distorts the evidence base in mental health research, leading to inflated estimates of treatment effects or risk associations. This is particularly critical for synthesizing evidence on mortality and life years lost among people with mental disorders, where accurate effect sizes are essential for public health prioritization. Existing bias-adjustment methods (e.g., trim-and-fill, selection models) often rely on strong, untestable assumptions and lack a formal framework to incorporate prior knowledge about the publication process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian state-space model, adapted from ecological population estimation, can robustly recover the underlying distribution of true effect sizes from a biased sample of published studies by explicitly modeling the latent population of all conducted studies and the observation process governing publication.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Bayesian state-space framework used in ecology for estimating animal abundance from imperfect surveys. The latent state will be the distribution of true study effect sizes (modeled hierarchically). The observation model will formalize the publication process as a function of p-value, result direction, and study size, with priors for publication probabilities informed by empirical studies like those by Ioannidis and Fanelli.
