Estimation will be performed using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan, applied to a case study meta-analysis of mortality risk associated with a specific mental disorder, leveraging Dr. Egger's expertise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, statistically rigorous tool for correcting publication bias in meta-analyses, moving beyond sensitivity analyses to direct estimation. It will deliver a more valid synthesis of evidence on the mortality burden of mental illness, directly informing Dr. Egger's research program. The open-source model implementation will be a methodological contribution applicable across epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical translation of a well-established modeling paradigm from ecology (state-space models for population dynamics) to the problem of publication bias, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously exploited. This approach naturally integrates hierarchical modeling of effects with a mechanistic model of study selection.

proposals.title: Bayesian adjustment for publication bias in meta-analyses of mortality and life years lost associated with mental illness
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses on mortality and life years lost in mental illness are crucial for public health policy but are vulnerable to publication bias, where small studies with non-significant results remain unpublished. This bias can lead to overestimation of effect sizes, misinforming resource allocation. Pre-2026, frequentist methods like trim-and-fill are common but have limitations in handling complex bias structures and incorporating prior evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis model, incorporating informative priors on the selection process and between-study heterogeneity, will provide more robust and less biased pooled estimates of mortality risk and years of life lost for people with mental disorders compared to standard frequentist approaches.
proposals.method: We will apply robust Bayesian meta-analysis methods, specifically selection models and models for small-study effects. Using Stan, we will implement hierarchical models where the likelihood of study publication is modeled as a function of its p-value or effect size precision. Informative priors for heterogeneity will be derived from Egger's prior cohort work and existing meta-epidemiological studies. The analysis will be applied to a systematic review of studies on mortality and life years lost for major depression and anxiety disorders.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological framework for more credible evidence synthesis in psychiatric epidemiology, directly applicable to Dr. Egger's research on life years lost. It will yield adjusted, more conservative estimates of the mortality burden of mental illness, strengthening the evidence base for clinical guidelines and health economic evaluations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored application of cutting-edge Bayesian selection models, pre-2026, to the specific domain of mortality in mental health, integrating substantive prior knowledge on heterogeneity from large cohort studies to improve bias adjustment where data are sparse.

proposals.title: A Simulation-to-Inference Framework for Quantifying Publication Bias in Meta-Analyses of Mortality and Mental Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Publication bias critically undermines the validity of meta-analyses, which are foundational for evidence-based medicine and public health policy. Existing bias detection tools, like funnel plots and Egger's test, rely on strong assumptions and offer limited, non-probabilistic quantification of bias severity. This is particularly problematic in fields like psychiatric epidemiology and mortality research, where Dr. Egger's work operates, as biased syntheses can misdirect clinical guidelines and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model trained on a comprehensive space of simulated meta-analytic datasets, incorporating known generative parameters for publication bias, can accurately recover the posterior distribution of the bias parameter from observed study summaries, providing a superior, uncertainty-aware quantification compared to traditional methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a flexible simulation engine that generates meta-analytic datasets with known, tunable levels of selection bias (e.g., via weight-function models), between-study heterogeneity, and study precision. A Bayesian neural network (BNN) will be trained on these simulations to map from vectors of observed effect sizes and standard errors to the posterior distribution of the publication bias parameter. The BNN's architecture will be designed to output parameters of a flexible distribution (e.g., a mixture model), capturing epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty. Validation will use held-out simulations and a curated set of real-world meta-analyses on mortality and mental health where external evidence (e.g., trial registries) provides a benchmark for bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for assessing publication bias risk with associated uncertainty, moving beyond binary detection to probabilistic estimation. It will directly enhance the rigor of Dr. Egger's research on life years lost and mental health outcomes by enabling more robust sensitivity analyses in future meta-analyses. The open-source framework will be applicable across biomedical and social sciences.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its use of a simulation-grounded, amortized Bayesian inference model specifically for publication bias estimation, replacing subjective shape recognition with a data-driven, probabilistic output. It integrates complex bias mechanisms and heterogeneity in a way that traditional regression-based tests cannot.

proposals.title: A small-sample correction for Egger's test: Development and validation for meta-analyses with 3-5 studies
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses in epidemiology and mental health research often include very few studies, especially for emerging questions or rare outcomes. Standard tests for publication bias, like Egger's regression test, are known to have inflated type I error rates and low power when applied to meta-analyses of fewer than 10 studies. This leads to unreliable conclusions about bias and effect validity in critical areas, such as estimating mortality or life years lost associated with mental illness from limited evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modified regression test, incorporating a small-sample variance correction and an exact reference distribution, will provide valid type I error control and improved power for detecting asymmetry in funnel plots based on only 3-5 studies.
proposals.method: We will develop a modified weighted linear regression of the standardized effect on its precision, using a refined variance estimator for the regression slope (e.g., a multiplicative or additive correction factor). The sampling distribution of the test statistic under the null hypothesis of symmetry will be derived via permutation or Monte Carlo simulation, conditioning on the observed standard errors, to create an exact or near-exact test. The method's performance (type I error, power) will be evaluated against the standard Egger's test and the Harbord test in an extensive simulation study covering typical effect measures (log odds ratios, mean differences) and heterogeneity scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, statistically rigorous tool for assessing publication bias in small meta-analyses, directly addressing a known methodological gap. It will enhance the reliability of evidence synthesis in fields like psychiatric epidemiology and infectious disease outcomes (e.g., candidaemia), where preliminary evidence is often based on a handful of studies.
proposals.novelty: While the problem is known, existing solutions remain ad-hoc or are validated for larger k. This proposal systematically develops and validates a dedicated correction and exact testing framework specifically for the extreme small-sample case (k<6), which is a common yet under-served scenario in applied research.

proposals.title: A GRADE-Adapted Framework for Grading Evidence from Observational Studies on Mortality and Life Years Lost in Mental Health
proposals.motivation: Observational studies, especially on mortality and life years lost among people with mental illness, are crucial for public health but vary widely in robustness. Current reporting often lacks a standardized, transparent assessment of evidence quality, hindering the translation of findings into policy and clinical guidelines. Adapting the established GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation) framework, designed for clinical guidelines, could provide a systematic, credible method to evaluate observational evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: A modified GRADE framework, incorporating domain-specific criteria for observational epidemiology (e.g., confounding control, outcome ascertainment, missing data handling), can produce a reliable and valid grading of evidence strength for mortality outcomes in mental health research.
proposals.method: We will develop a modified GRADE checklist by integrating core GRADE domains (risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, publication bias) with key observational study concerns, such as residual confounding assessment using quantitative bias analysis and E-value calculations. The framework will be piloted and validated on a curated sample of pre-2025 cohort studies (including Egger's prior work) investigating mortality and life years lost among patients with mental health conditions. Inter-rater reliability will be assessed, and the resulting evidence grades will be compared against expert consensus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, standardized tool for authors, reviewers, and guideline developers to critically appraise and transparently report the quality of evidence from observational mental health studies. It will enhance the credibility of epidemiological findings and facilitate their integration into health policy and resource allocation decisions.
proposals.novelty: While GRADE is widely used for interventional studies, its systematic adaptation and operationalization for the specific methodological challenges of observational research on mortality and life years lost represents a novel application. This moves beyond generic checklists by embedding quantitative bias assessment directly into the grading process.

proposals.title: Mendelian Randomization for Clinical Trial Enrichment: A Framework for Designing Efficient Trials in Neuropsychiatry
proposals.motivation: Clinical trials for neuropsychiatric disorders and their somatic comorbidities are often large, lengthy, and costly due to heterogeneous patient populations and modest treatment effects. This inefficiency delays the delivery of effective interventions to patients. Pre-2026, Mendelian Randomization (MR) has matured as a method for causal inference in epidemiology, but its potential to directly inform and streamline trial design remains largely untapped.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetic instruments identified through MR studies can be used to define a high-likelihood subgroup of patients for whom a specific biological pathway is causative, thereby enriching clinical trial populations and increasing statistical power to detect a treatment effect.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will perform MR analyses using large-scale genetic consortia data (e.g., UK Biobank, psychiatric genomics consortia) to identify robust genetic predictors (instrumental variables) for a modifiable risk factor (e.g., C-reactive protein) linked to a clinical outcome (e.g., depression-related mortality). Second, we will design a simulated trial protocol where eligibility is contingent on a polygenic score derived from these instruments. We will use statistical simulation to compare the required sample size and power of this genetically enriched trial against a traditional design, employing bootstrap and Monte Carlo methods to estimate confidence intervals for the treatment effect in the enriched cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, genetically-informed methodology for clinical trial enrichment, directly translating population genetic findings into practical trial design. It will offer a formal framework for designing smaller, faster, and more targeted trials, particularly for disorders within Dr. Egger's research themes of mental health and mortality. The output will be a validated simulation tool and reporting guideline for MR-based trial enrichment.
proposals.novelty: While MR is established for causal discovery, its application as a direct tool for *prospective* clinical trial design and patient stratification is novel. This moves beyond observational correlation to actively shaping interventional study cohorts based on genetic causal evidence.

proposals.title: A Formal Evidentiary Triage and Calibration Framework for Estimating Mortality and Life Years Lost in Mental Health: Application to a South African Insurance Cohort
proposals.motivation: In fields like mental health epidemiology, causal inference is often based on observational data from insurance claims or registries, which are subject to severe selection bias, confounding, and measurement error. Pre-2026, standard methods either ignore these biases or discard valuable data, leading to overconfident or uninformative conclusions. A systematic framework is urgently needed to responsibly use such flawed evidence to inform policy while explicitly quantifying its limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically mapping bias structures and restricting inference to bias-robust questions, we can derive plausible bounds for mortality and life years lost associated with mental illness that are more reliable and explicitly uncertain than conventional point estimates from the same data.
proposals.method: We will apply the framework to a South African medical insurance cohort. First, we will conduct a diagnostic bias audit, mapping sources (e.g., healthy insurer bias, differential mortality coding) using directed acyclic graphs. The core method will use pre-2026 quantitative bias analysis, including probabilistic bias correction and Monte Carlo sensitivity analyses, to derive bounded estimates. We will then compute a 'confidence deficit' score, integrating the variance from bias parameters with conventional statistical uncertainty, calibrated against a benchmark of high-quality evidence where available.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a practical, transparent tool for evidence-based decision-making in mental health policy under severe data constraints. It will advance methodological rigor in observational epidemiology by replacing single estimates with calibrated bounds and an explicit research agenda to fill evidence gaps.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing sensitivity analyses, this framework formally integrates bias diagnostics, inference restriction, and a calibrated confidence metric into a unified workflow for evidence-poor fields, shifting the goal from finding a definitive answer to responsibly managing evidentiary uncertainty.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Meta-Analytic Framework for Evidence Synthesis When Low-Risk-of-Bias Trials Are Unavailable: Application to Mortality Outcomes in Mental and Somatic Health
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses often exclude high-risk-of-bias (RoB) trials, potentially discarding all available evidence in fields like complex mental health or long-term mortality interventions where low-bias RCTs are scarce. This creates an evidence gap, leaving clinicians and policymakers without synthesized guidance. A formal methodology is needed to quantitatively integrate and interpret evidence from studies with varying, but predominantly high, RoB.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian hierarchical model, which explicitly incorporates RoB assessments as informative priors on study-specific bias parameters, can produce more accurate and usable pooled effect estimates than complete exclusion of high-RoB studies, while providing a transparent quantification of residual uncertainty due to bias.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian multivariate meta-regression model where the RoB domains (e.g., from Cochrane's RoB 2.0 tool) inform prior distributions for study-level bias terms. These bias terms will adjust the observed effects towards a latent 'true' effect, with the adjustment's magnitude informed by empirical distributions of bias impact from previous meta-epidemiological studies. The model will be implemented in Stan, allowing for full posterior inference on the adjusted effect, the contribution of each bias domain, and the predicted effect under a hypothetical low-bias trial scenario.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, statistically rigorous framework for evidence synthesis in research areas lacking high-quality RCTs, moving beyond the binary inclusion/exclusion paradigm. It will directly contribute to more informed guidelines in mental health and complex somatic conditions (e.g., post-COVID sequelae) by making the best possible use of existing, albeit imperfect, trial data.
proposals.novelty: While bias-adjustment models exist, our approach innovates by directly integrating standardized RoB tool assessments into a Bayesian prior structure for synthesis, and by validating the framework against cohort data (where robust observational evidence exists) to calibrate bias parameters, a method not commonly applied in this context.

proposals.title: Accounting for Non-Independent Bias in Meta-Analysis: A Spatiotemporal Modeling Approach for Evidence Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses are foundational to evidence-based medicine but rely on the critical assumption that biases across included studies are independent. This assumption is likely violated, as trials conducted by the same research group or in the same era may share systematic methodological flaws or contextual pressures, such as those observed during the COVID-19 pandemic. Ignoring this autocorrelation can lead to overconfident, and potentially misleading, pooled estimates of treatment effects and mortality outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bias in clinical trials is an autocorrelated process across dimensions like research group affiliation and publication year, and that explicitly modeling this structure using hierarchical spatiotemporal models will significantly alter pooled effect estimates and their uncertainty compared to standard meta-analytic techniques.
proposals.method: We will adapt geostatistical and longitudinal modeling frameworks to the meta-analytic context. For temporal autocorrelation (e.g., trials published in close succession), we will implement Gaussian process priors with a kernel (e.g., squared-exponential) over publication year within a Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis model. For spatial/network autocorrelation among research groups, we will employ conditional autoregressive (CAR) or autoregressive random effects models, defining proximity based on shared authorship or institutional affiliation. Models will be fitted using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo in Stan, applied to existing meta-analytic datasets from our prior work on mortality and mental health.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for quantifying and adjusting for non-independent bias in evidence synthesis, moving beyond simple risk-of-bias stratification. It will yield more realistic uncertainty intervals for pooled estimates and offer new tools for sensitivity analysis, directly improving the reliability of syntheses that inform clinical guidelines and public health policy.
proposals.novelty: While meta-epidemiological studies assess bias, they treat trials as independent units. This proposal is novel in formally conceptualizing and modeling the research landscape as a correlated field, applying spatial and temporal autocorrelation models—common in geography and ecology—to the problem of bias propagation in meta-analysis for the first time.

proposals.title: Development and validation of outcome-specific risk-of-bias (RoB) tools for observational studies on mortality and mental health: a multi-method study
proposals.motivation: Current generic risk-of-bias tools, like ROBINS-I, are applied uniformly across studies but may inadequately capture bias sources specific to different outcome types. For instance, mortality (objective, routinely recorded) and patient-reported mental health outcomes (subjective, self-assessed) are susceptible to distinct biases in measurement and confounding. This lack of specificity can lead to inconsistent evidence syntheses, particularly in fields like psychiatric epidemiology and life-years-lost research where outcome measurement is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that outcome-specific risk-of-bias assessment tools, developed through expert consensus and empirical validation, will demonstrate superior reliability, feasibility, and predictive validity for bias in effect estimates compared to generic tools when applied to observational studies of mortality and mental health outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods approach. First, we will conduct systematic reviews and Delphi surveys with methodologies and content experts (e.g., in mental health, mortality registries) to identify outcome-specific bias domains and signaling questions. Second, we will develop prototype tools for two key domains: (1) all-cause/ cause-specific mortality and (2) patient-reported mental health outcomes. Third, we will validate these tools in an empirical study using a pre-2026 cohort of existing studies (e.g., from Egger's prior work on life years lost and mental illness). Validation will involve inter-rater reliability assessment and by linking RoB ratings to quantitative bias indicators using multivariate regression, adapting methods from bias-adjustment meta-analysis.
proposals.contribution: This project will provide the first rigorously developed, outcome-tailored RoB tools for two major outcome classes, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach. It will directly improve the rigor of evidence appraisal in systematic reviews and meta-analyses concerning mortality and mental health, leading to more accurate estimates of burden and treatment effects. The methodological framework will be generalizable for developing tools for other outcome types.
proposals.novelty: While methodological research has focused on improving RoB tools for study designs, the explicit tailoring to outcome type—addressing the unique bias pathways of objective administrative data versus subjective patient-reported data—is a novel and necessary advancement not yet systematically undertaken in pre-2026 tool development.

proposals.title: Empirical quantification of bias magnitude in observational studies of mental illness: extending evidence to objective non-mortality outcomes
proposals.motivation: Observational studies on mental illness and health outcomes are susceptible to confounding and selection bias, but the magnitude of bias is often unknown. While prior work, including Egger's on life years lost, has focused on mortality, bias for other critical objective outcomes—like healthcare utilization, specific morbidities, and functional status—remains unquantified. This gap limits the interpretation of a vast body of epidemiological evidence and hinders the design of more robust studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the average magnitude of bias (e.g., from unmeasured confounding) in observational studies linking mental illness to objective, non-mortality outcomes is substantial and varies systematically by outcome category and study design. This bias can be empirically estimated by comparing results from well-conducted observational studies with benchmark estimates from quasi-experimental or linked trial data.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-epidemiological study. First, we will systematically identify paired estimates for the association between mental illness (e.g., depression, anxiety) and outcomes including hospital admissions, incidence of cardiovascular disease, and employment status. Each pair will consist of an estimate from a conventional observational study and a benchmark from a study using stronger causal methods (e.g., Mendelian randomization, instrumental variable analyses, or within-pair designs from twin/ sibling registries). Using multivariate meta-regression, we will model the ratio of odds ratios (ROR) or difference in effects as a function of outcome category, study quality indicators, and data source, quantifying the average bias and its predictors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical estimates of bias magnitude for a range of objective, policy-relevant outcomes in mental health epidemiology. It will produce a bias 'correction' framework to inform the interpretation of existing literature and improve the planning of future studies by identifying outcome domains where particular methodological rigor is needed. The results will directly complement Egger's work on mortality by building a more complete map of evidence reliability.
proposals.novelty: This extends bias quantification beyond mortality and subjective health to understudied objective outcomes, using a paired-design meta-epidemiological approach that directly contrasts conventional and quasi-experimental estimates within the same research question, which is novel for mental health research.

proposals.title: Joint Bias Amplification: A Multi-Domain Interaction Analysis of Bias Domains on Mortality Effect Estimates in Observational Mental Health Studies
proposals.motivation: Risk of bias (RoB) tools like ROBINS-I assess bias domains (e.g., confounding, selection, measurement) independently. However, in observational epidemiology, especially in mental health and mortality research, these biases likely interact, potentially amplifying or dampening their individual effects on estimates. Understanding these interactions is crucial for accurate evidence synthesis and for guiding the design of future studies, such as those on life-years-lost in psychiatric populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of bias domains (e.g., high confounding bias combined with high measurement bias) have a supra-additive, interactive effect on the magnitude and direction of mortality risk estimates in observational mental health research, beyond the sum of their individual impacts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-epidemiological study using a curated database of pre-2025 observational studies on mortality among people with mental disorders. For each study, two independent reviewers will assess RoB across key domains using a modified ROBINS-I framework. We will employ multivariate meta-regression and Bayesian network analysis to model the joint influence of bias domain combinations (e.g., confounding*selection, blinding*allocation) on reported hazard ratios. Interaction terms will be explicitly tested to identify significant synergistic or antagonistic effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence on how bias domains interact, moving beyond additive models. It will produce a refined framework for grading evidence certainty in systematic reviews (e.g., GRADE) that accounts for bias interactions. The findings will directly inform the design and critical appraisal of future cohort studies in mental and public health epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While bias domains are assessed separately, their statistical interaction is rarely modeled. This proposal explicitly tests for non-additive, combinatorial effects, a conceptual advance over standard RoB tools that treat domains as independent.

proposals.title: Prospective Validation of Empirical Bias Estimates in Meta-Analyses: A Hindcasting Approach Using Trial Registry Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Empirical bias estimates, such as those derived from small-study effects or comparison with large trials, are crucial for adjusting meta-analyses and informing policy. However, their validity is typically assessed retrospectively on the same data used to generate them, risking overfitting and limiting confidence in their generalizability to future research. A prospective, out-of-sample test is needed to establish their predictive validity, analogous to model validation in other predictive sciences.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-specified formulas for estimating bias in meta-analyses, derived from historical trial data, will prospectively predict the direction and magnitude of bias observed in future, independent cohorts of completed clinical trials.
proposals.method: We will design a prospective cohort study of clinical trials registered in the WHO ICTRP or ClinicalTrials.gov after a pre-defined date (e.g., Jan 1, 2025). For a set of prioritized medical interventions, we will pre-register the exact algorithms (e.g., Egger regression, limit meta-analysis, comparison with subsequent large trials) and parameters for generating bias estimates. We will then actively monitor these trial registries, identify completed trials, and track their publication and inclusion in future meta-analyses. After a sufficient follow-up period (e.g., 3-5 years), we will formally compare the pre-registered bias predictions against the observed biases in the new meta-analytic cohorts, using metrics of calibration and discrimination.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, out-of-sample validation of empirical bias estimation methods, moving beyond cross-validation. It will establish a new benchmark for methodological rigor in meta-research and provide evidence-based guidance on which bias adjustment methods are most reliable for future evidence synthesis. The prospective registry cohort design creates a replicable framework for the ongoing evaluation of meta-analytic methodologies.
proposals.novelty: The approach adapts the hindcasting validation paradigm from climate science to meta-research, transforming bias estimation from a retrospective diagnostic into a testable predictive model. This shifts the validation standard from replication in historical splits to genuine prospective prediction against future data.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Bias Hazard Score for Clinical Trials: A Machine Learning Framework Adapted from Seismic Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Systematic bias in clinical trials distorts evidence synthesis, leading to flawed guidelines and suboptimal patient care. Current bias assessment tools like Cochrane's Risk of Bias 2 are applied post-hoc, after trials are published, and rely on labor-intensive manual review. A predictive, quantitative tool that estimates bias risk from trial metadata at the point of registration or publication would revolutionize evidence surveillance and prioritization for systematic reviewers and regulators.
proposals.hypothesis: That features of clinical trial metadata—such as design, funding source, investigator history, and registry completeness—can be used to train a machine learning model to probabilistically predict the presence and potential magnitude of bias, analogous to how geological features predict seismic hazard.
proposals.method: We will curate a labeled dataset by linking trial records from ClinicalTrials.gov and WHO ICTRP to bias assessments in Cochrane reviews (e.g., RoB2 judgments). We will engineer features from metadata, including funding type (industry vs. non-industry), sample size, randomization method, blinding, country income level, and investigator publication history. Using this dataset, we will train and validate gradient boosting models (XGBoost/LightGBM) and neural networks to output a continuous 'bias hazard' score and a classification of bias risk level. Model performance will be evaluated using area under the ROC curve and calibration metrics.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first validated, automated tool for pre-emptive bias risk prediction, enabling rapid screening of large trial corpora. It will provide systematic reviewers and meta-analysts with a quantitative metric to prioritize studies for in-depth evaluation and to potentially weight evidence in sensitivity analyses, thereby improving the efficiency and reliability of evidence synthesis.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual adaptation of probabilistic hazard modeling from geophysics to the domain of research integrity. Methodologically, it moves beyond post-hoc classification to predictive risk scoring based on accessible trial metadata, a paradigm shift from current manual assessment tools.

proposals.title: Ensemble Bias Adjustment for Evidence Synthesis: An Empirical Calibration and Model-Averaging Framework Applied to Mortality and Life Years Lost in Mental Health Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Systematic bias in observational and meta-analytic studies, such as selection bias and unmeasured confounding, critically undermines the validity of pooled effect estimates, particularly in fields like psychiatric epidemiology where longitudinal mortality outcomes are studied. Pre-2026 methods for bias adjustment, like survey weighting or single propensity score models, are often applied ad-hoc without robust empirical validation of their performance under known bias structures, leading to uncertain and potentially misleading conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble framework that empirically calibrates multiple bias-adjustment models on simulated data with known bias and then applies model averaging based on cross-validated performance will produce more accurate and reliable pooled effect estimates for real-world evidence synthesis than reliance on any single correction method.
proposals.method: We will generate a library of simulated meta-analyses with pre-specified bias structures (e.g., publication bias, confounding by severity) mirroring mental health cohort data. We will empirically test the performance of candidate adjustment methods, including inverse probability weighting, trim-and-fill, and regression-based corrections. For real-data application (e.g., synthesizing studies on life years lost among patients with mental illness), we will implement a stacking ensemble or Bayesian model averaging approach, where the weight given to each adjusted estimate is determined by its cross-validated predictive performance on the simulated calibration set, propagating model uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, transparent, and empirically grounded framework for bias adjustment in meta-analysis, moving beyond heuristic choices. It will deliver a practical tool for synthesizing evidence in areas like psychiatric mortality, where bias is pervasive, and offer a formal way to quantify and communicate the uncertainty introduced by the correction process itself.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, simulation-based calibration of bias-adjustment methods and the subsequent model-averaged application, which directly addresses the model selection problem inherent in bias correction—a step not systematized in pre-2026 evidence synthesis methodology.

proposals.title: Inverse Probability of Selection Weighting (IPSW) for bias correction in meta-analyses of mortality and life years lost: a simulation and applied study
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses, especially of observational studies on mortality and mental health, are vulnerable to selection and reporting biases, threatening the validity of pooled estimates. Traditional methods like funnel plots and Egger's test detect but do not quantitatively adjust for these biases. This limits the accuracy of synthesized evidence used to inform public health policy and clinical guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying survey sampling weighting techniques, specifically Inverse Probability of Selection Weighting (IPSW), to studies in a meta-analysis can effectively rebalance the sample to represent a target population of all conducted studies, thereby reducing bias in the pooled effect estimate.
proposals.method: We will adapt IPSW, where the probability of a study's inclusion in the meta-analysis is modeled based on observable characteristics (e.g., study size, design quality, publication year, region). Weights will be derived from a logistic regression model or a propensity score model, with the target 'population' defined via a comprehensive register (e.g., clinicaltrials.gov) or a benchmark of high-quality cohort studies. The performance of IPSW-adjusted meta-analysis will be evaluated against standard random-effects models using simulation studies with known bias mechanisms and applied to a pre-2026 dataset on life years lost associated with mental illness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative adjustment method to correct for systematic bias in meta-analytic summaries, moving beyond mere detection. It will deliver a practical framework and open-source software (e.g., an R package) for implementing IPSW in meta-analysis, directly enhancing the reliability of evidence synthesis in epidemiology and health services research.
proposals.novelty: While survey weighting is standard in population surveys, its formal application to correct for non-random study inclusion in meta-analysis is novel. This approach directly addresses the data-generating mechanism of meta-biases, offering a more principled correction than existing statistical adjustments.

proposals.title: Automated, large-scale assessment of risk of bias in clinical trials: a machine learning approach applied to global COVID-19 and mental health research
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses, crucial for evidence-based medicine, are limited by the manual, time-consuming nature of risk-of-bias (RoB) assessment, creating a bottleneck for timely evidence synthesis. This is particularly critical for rapidly evolving fields like COVID-19 research and for synthesizing evidence on long-term outcomes, such as mortality among people with mental illness, where large volumes of trials exist. An automated, scalable system is needed to efficiently evaluate the evidence base and identify systematic weaknesses in study design and reporting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised machine learning model, trained on human-assessed RoB judgments from the Cochrane Database, can accurately and reliably automate the assessment of RoB for clinical trials in large bibliographic databases, with performance generalizable across medical domains including infectious diseases and psychiatry.
proposals.method: We will develop a natural language processing (NLP) pipeline using pre-2026 transformer architectures (e.g., BERT, BioBERT) fine-tuned for sequence classification. Training data will be derived from published Cochrane reviews, pairing trial text (abstracts, methods sections from PubMed) with human-coded RoB judgments for domains like randomization and blinding. The model will be validated on held-out Cochrane data and then applied at scale to trials in the WHO's International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP) and PubMed, focusing initially on COVID-19 and mental health intervention trials. Performance will be benchmarked against manual assessments by trained reviewers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, domain-adaptable tool for automated RoB assessment at a global scale, enabling rapid evidence quality surveillance. It will directly contribute to more efficient systematic reviews and allow for large-scale analyses of how trial quality correlates with reported outcomes and evolves over time, building on Prof. Egger's expertise in life years lost and cohort studies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored automation for specific RoB domains, this proposal integrates a robust, pre-trained NLP model with a uniquely comprehensive training dataset from Cochrane to create a generalizable tool for end-to-end, multi-domain RoB assessment across vast trial registries and literature databases.

proposals.title: Optimizing Observational Study Design for Mortality and Life-Year Outcomes: An Inverse Bias Modelling Approach
proposals.motivation: Observational studies on mortality and life-years lost, such as those in mental health and post-COVID-19 outcomes, are highly susceptible to confounding and selection bias. Pre-2026 methods focus on quantifying bias post-hoc, but this reactive approach cannot guarantee robust findings. A proactive framework to design studies that are inherently resilient to known bias structures is critically needed to improve causal inference in epidemiology.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting established bias prediction models, it is possible to computationally generate optimized observational study protocols (defining inclusion criteria, covariate adjustment sets, and sampling strategies) that minimize the expected bias for specific outcome types, particularly mortality and life-years lost.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse optimization algorithm building on pre-2026 causal diagrams and quantitative bias analysis. Using a simulation engine, we will parameterize bias models (e.g., for unmeasured confounding, selection bias) based on Egger's prior work on mortality. The core algorithm will treat the study protocol parameters as decision variables and use a Monte Carlo approach combined with gradient-free optimization (e.g., simulated annealing) to identify protocol configurations that minimize a composite bias score for the target outcome.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, practical tool for epidemiologists to pre-emptively strengthen study designs before data collection, increasing the validity of findings in mental and physical health research. It will formalize the link between bias quantification and design, moving the field from assessment to active mitigation.
proposals.novelty: While bias quantification is standard, the inverse problem—systematically deriving robust designs from bias estimates—is largely unexplored. This shifts the paradigm from diagnosing bias in completed studies to engineering resilience into studies at their inception.

ideas.title: Empirically Calibrated and Model-Averaged Bias Adjustment for Evidence Synthesis
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond applying a single weighting method by embedding bias adjustment within a rigorous calibration and selection framework. It uses simulated meta-analyses with known bias structures to empirically test the performance and potential pitfalls of various correction methods, including survey-style weighting. The system then applies an ensemble method to real data, weighting the results from multiple candidate adjustment models based on their cross-validated performance, thereby robustly acknowledging and propagating model uncertainty inherent in bias correction.

ideas.title: Cross-Validate Empirical Bias Estimates Using Prospective Registry Cohorts
ideas.description: This idea adapts a validation paradigm from climate science, where models are tested against future, out-of-sample data. The analogous solution is hindcasting, where a climate model's skill is evaluated by seeing how well its projections match later observations. Adapted to this context, the paper's method for generating empirical bias estimates is treated as a predictive model. A prospective study is designed where the bias estimation formulas are pre-specified and applied to a newly defined cohort of trials (e.g., those registered after a certain date). Once these new trials are completed and their results published in subsequent meta-analyses, the pre-registered bias predictions are formally compared against the actual observed biases. This provides a robust, prospective test of the generalizability and predictive validity of the original bias estimation framework, moving beyond simple replication in a different historical sample.

ideas.title: Adapting particle physics meta-analysis methods for small-study-dominated test evaluation
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to solve the problem of evaluating a modified test's properties in meta-analyses dominated by numerous, very small studies by drawing an analogy from particle physics. In that field, discoveries are often made by combining many independent experimental runs or data channels, each with very few observed events (analogous to small sample sizes). The analogous solution involves sophisticated statistical combination methods like the CLs method and BLUE estimators, which build a global likelihood model that meticulously accounts for the uncertainty and potential systematic biases of each small 'trial' to produce a robust combined result. Adapted to this research context, the idea is to develop a specialized meta-analytic framework for test evaluation. This framework would treat each small primary study as a low-information unit, explicitly modeling its error structure and risk of bias within a combined statistical model. The outcome would be a rigorous method for estimating the test's true properties and assessing the overall evidence strength, providing a solution tailored to a research landscape flooded with small, underpowered studies, much as particle physics handles data from many low-significance experiments.

ideas.title: Predicting Trial Bias via a Seismic Hazard Assessment Framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysics, where probabilistic models predict seismic hazard from historical and geological metadata, to the problem of predicting bias in clinical trials. In seismology, models are trained on features like fault lines and past quakes to forecast the likelihood and magnitude of future events. Similarly, we propose training machine learning models on a curated corpus of clinical trial metadata—including design characteristics, funding details, and investigator profiles—linked to known bias assessments from sources like Cochrane reviews. The adapted model would output a probabilistic 'bias hazard' score for any new trial, predicting both the risk that bias is present and the estimated magnitude of its distorting effect on the trial's results, thereby providing a quantitative, pre-emptive risk assessment tool for evidence synthesis and regulatory oversight.

ideas.title: Causal Simulation-Grounded Estimation of Publication Bias from Meta-Analytic Data
ideas.description: This system first employs a flexible simulation engine that generates meta-analytic datasets with known, parameterized levels of publication bias, heterogeneity, and other confounding factors. A machine learning model (e.g., a Bayesian neural network) is trained on these simulated datasets to predict the posterior distribution of the publication bias parameter directly from the study effect sizes and standard errors. The final model is validated on held-out simulated data and, cautiously, on curated real-world meta-analyses with strong external evidence regarding bias, providing a quantitative, uncertainty-aware estimate of publication bias risk that moves beyond subjective shape recognition.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian state-space modeling from ecology to adjust for publication bias in meta-analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from population ecology, where Bayesian state-space models are routinely used to estimate true population parameters from biased observational data. The analogous problem in ecology involves estimating true animal abundance from surveys that have imperfect detection and systematic sampling biases. Ecologists solve this by building hierarchical models that separate the latent state process (true population dynamics) from the observation process (biased sampling), using informative priors based on known detection probabilities. Adapted to meta-analysis, the core research idea is to treat the distribution of true effect sizes across all conducted studies (published and unpublished) as the latent state process. The observed meta-analytic dataset of published studies then becomes the observation process, which is modeled with an explicit publication bias mechanism—where the probability a study enters the dataset depends on its p-value, results direction, and study characteristics. By using informative priors on this publication probability function (e.g., based on empirical studies of publication bias like the 'file drawer' problem), a Bayesian hierarchical model can be constructed to estimate the adjusted, underlying effect size distribution, directly correcting for the documented biases in the scientific literature.

ideas.title: Challenge that all RCT evidence is equally reliable

ideas.title: Modeling Bias as a Spatially or Temporally Autocorrelated Process in Meta-Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core meta-epidemiological assumption of bias independence across trials by drawing an analogy from geostatistics. In spatial fields, measurements are correlated based on proximity, solved by models like kriging that use covariance structures. Adapted to research synthesis, we propose treating the 'research landscape' as having dimensions like group affiliation and publication year. Bias is modeled as an autocorrelated process, where trials from the same lab or from temporally close eras share correlated bias components. Implementing this requires advanced hierarchical meta-analysis models with structured random effects—such as Gaussian process priors over time or conditional autoregressive structures for research groups—to explicitly estimate and adjust for the non-independence of biases, fundamentally altering how we quantify and propagate uncertainty in evidence synthesis.

ideas.title: Adapting Spatial Autocorrelation Tests for Meta-Analytic Dependence
ideas.description: The original idea is to challenge the assumption of independence among study estimates when testing for publication bias. Drawing an analogy from spatial statistics, where the non-independence of geographically proximate data points (spatial autocorrelation) is a well-known problem solved by tests like Moran's I and subsequent covariance-adjustment models. The adapted research idea is to develop analogous diagnostic tests for meta-analytic dependence. By defining a 'proximity' or 'overlap' matrix between studies (based on shared authors, institutions, patient populations, or methodological features), one can calculate a dependency statistic. This formally tests the independence assumption underlying standard publication bias tests (e.g., Egger's regression). Furthermore, the estimated dependency structure can be integrated into bias-correction models, such as modified selection models or multivariate meta-regressions, to provide valid inference even when study estimates are correlated.

ideas.title: Evidentiary Triage and Calibration Framework for Evidence-Poor Fields
ideas.description: This research develops a formal system for fields where no low-bias trials exist, focusing on responsible evidence use rather than simply analyzing flawed data. It first diagnostically maps the sources and severity of bias across the available evidence corpus. The core method then restricts inference to questions the biased data can partially address, such as deriving plausible bounds for effects or identifying robust patterns across bias structures. All outputs are paired with a calibrated 'confidence deficit' score that quantifies the increased uncertainty due to missing ideal evidence, explicitly directing users toward evidence gap filling and hedging against overconfident conclusions.

ideas.title: Design-space bias susceptibility mapping
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond unverifiable single-trial predictions to a pre-registered, simulation-based framework for assessing an experiment's susceptibility to bias. For a planned study design, it explicitly defines a set of plausible bias mechanisms (e.g., non-random attrition, observer bias) and uses Monte Carlo simulations to generate a distribution of possible results under these biased conditions. The output is a 'bias susceptibility map' that visually and quantitatively shows how robust the design is and which potential biases pose the greatest threat, guiding both design improvements and more nuanced interpretation of the eventual single-trial result.

ideas.title: Causal simulation of trial design to objectively quantify bias risk
ideas.description: This system constructs a causal model representing how key trial design choices (like blinding, allocation concealment, comparator selection) influence outcome distributions through known bias pathways. Instead of training only on historical RoB labels, it uses semi-synthetic data generation to simulate trials with and without specific bias-inducing flaws, comparing outcome distributions to quantify the likely magnitude of bias for a given design. The output is an objective, interpretable risk profile that shows which design elements contribute most to potential bias and allows for the testing of proposed design fixes before a trial is finalized.

ideas.title: Specification-Robust Multiverse Meta-Analysis for Publication Bias Adjustment
ideas.description: This method pre-registers a comprehensive space of Bayesian hierarchical models encompassing diverse, plausible mechanisms for publication bias and a range of prior distributions. It conducts a multiverse analysis across this entire specification space, rather than relying on a single chosen model. The final inference is generated through Bayesian model averaging across the multiverse, producing an estimate and uncertainty interval that explicitly account for uncertainty in how publication bias and prior knowledge are modeled. This approach directly quantifies and controls for specification sensitivity, making the adjusted meta-analytic conclusion robust to analyst degrees of freedom in model selection.

ideas.title: Invert the primary efficacy versus acceptability trade-off
ideas.description: Prioritize finding drugs with the best acceptability first, then rank them for efficacy within that constraint.
ideas.sourceTitle: Comparative Efficacy and Acceptability of 21 Antidepressant Drugs for the Acute Treatment of Adults With Major Depressive Disorder: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-Analysis.

ideas.title: The Observer Effect in Clinical Trials: Outcome Selection as a Determinant of Measurement Bias
ideas.description: This idea inverts the typical causality by proposing that the choice of outcome type influences the likelihood of introducing bias in trial conduct, rather than bias merely being an independent threat to a pre-selected outcome. The analogy is drawn from quantum physics and measurement science, specifically the 'observer effect,' where the choice of what to measure (position or momentum) determines the nature of the disturbance to the system. The analogous solution involves selecting measurement techniques to minimize this inherent disturbance. Adapted to clinical research, this leads to the idea of systematically classifying outcome types (e.g., clinician-assessed, patient-reported, hard endpoint, surrogate) based on their inherent 'vulnerability profile' to specific biases like performance or detection bias. The goal is to create a predictive, causal model and decision-support tool that allows trial designers to foresee and mitigate bias risks by making strategic, bias-minimizing choices at the outcome selection stage, fundamentally reframing outcome selection as a primary bias control mechanism.

ideas.title: Adapting Paleontological Inference Methods for Systematic Reviews with High-RoB Evidence
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that low-bias trials are always available by adapting solutions from paleontology, a field that fundamentally operates with an incomplete and biased evidence base. Paleontologists use techniques like phylogenetic bracketing and model-based inference to reconstruct evolutionary histories from fragmented fossils. Adapted to systematic reviews, this involves developing novel methodological frameworks that allow for inference when all available trials are at high or unclear risk of bias. Instead of halting the review, these methods would enable researchers to 'bracket' potential effects based on mechanistic pathways of related interventions, stratify evidence by type of bias to model its influence, and produce explicitly uncertain but informed estimates. The output would be a qualified, transparent inference about an intervention's effect, accompanied by a clear description of the evidence limitations—similar to a paleontologist's reconstruction of a dinosaur with noted missing parts.

ideas.title: Adapting Financial Portfolio Risk Models to Meta-Analysis Bias Detection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from quantitative finance, where copula models are used to capture the complex, often nonlinear dependence between asset returns and their volatilities for portfolio risk management. The analogous problem in meta-analysis is modeling the joint distribution of an effect size estimate and its variance, which are theoretically linked but can exhibit irregular dependence due to selective reporting and bias. By applying a copula-based framework—specifically selecting copulas that capture lower-tail dependence (where small-variance studies have inflated effects)—we can create a model to simulate the expected joint distribution under no bias. Deviations from this model, particularly an over-concentration of studies in the region of high effect and low variance, can be quantitatively flagged as evidence of systematic bias, offering a more nuanced detection method than existing funnel-plot or regression-based techniques.

ideas.title: A Copula-Embedded Selection Model with Identification Strategies for Disentangling Bias from Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This approach embeds a flexible copula model within a formal selection model to directly represent how publication bias induces dependence between effect size and variance. To overcome the identifiability flaw, the system incorporates specific identifying strategies, such as using studies with pre-registered designs as an instrumental variable for the selection process or analyzing the distribution of p-values around thresholds. This allows the joint model to separately estimate the parameters governing genuine heterogeneity and those governing selection bias, providing a more robust and interpretable detection of bias than a standalone dependence measure.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Infer methods from poor reports

ideas.title: Distinguishing True Signal from Rare-Event Artifacts via Calibrated Null Hypothesis Testing
ideas.description: This research idea adapts signal detection methods from particle physics to the problem of distinguishing genuine small-study effects (like publication bias) from meta-analytic artifacts caused solely by rare, high-impact events in small studies. In particle physics, a precise background model is built to calculate the exact probability that a perceived signal is just a rare fluctuation. Similarly, here we would construct a calibrated null model that simulates the distribution of asymmetry metrics (e.g., from funnel plots or regression tests) when the only source of asymmetry is the inherent, random chance of extreme results occurring in small samples. The adapted method 'inverts the test' by requiring that observed asymmetry be inconsistent with this 'rare-event artifact' model to claim evidence for a true small-study effect. This provides a more rigorous filter against false positives in meta-analysis.

ideas.title: Sequential Copula-Based Change-Point Detection for Meta-Analytic Bias Surveillance
ideas.description: This research direction integrates copula models and time-series change-point detection to monitor the evolution of meta-analytic evidence for bias. As studies are added sequentially, a chosen copula models the joint distribution of effect size and its variance at each step, and a change-point algorithm monitors the copula parameters (e.g., dependence strength, tail behavior) over time. A detected change-point signals the introduction of studies with a systematically different and suspect dependence structure, providing a statistically principled alert for bias infiltration. This allows for real-time bias diagnosis by linking the altered copula shape at the change-point to specific mechanisms like publication bias or p-hacking.

ideas.title: Calibrating Meta-Analysis via Seismic Tomography Back-Projection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from seismic tomography, where wave travel-time asymmetries are inverted to correct subsurface models, to the context of meta-analysis scaling. The core innovation is to frame funnel plot asymmetry or other detected imbalances not just as a test for bias, but as the input data for an inverse problem. Analogous to how seismologists use travel-time residuals to back-project and correct a velocity model, we propose developing a model that inverts the pattern of observed study effect sizes and their variances to estimate the latent selection process or bias function. The solution would iteratively adjust the initial pooled estimate until the expected asymmetry under the bias model matches the observed asymmetry, thereby scaling the analysis to correct, not just flag, the systematic distortion.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Covariance Shrinkage for High-Dimensional, Correlated Binary Meta-Analysis
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to scale up tests for meta-analyses with multiple correlated binary outcomes, a high-dimensional problem where estimating within-study correlation structures is challenging with few studies. Drawing an analogy from quantitative finance, where stabilizing high-dimensional covariance matrices for asset returns is critical, the analogous solution is covariance matrix shrinkage (e.g., Ledoit-Wolf). This method optimally combines a noisy sample covariance matrix with a well-structured, low-dimensional target. Adapted to this research context, the idea is to develop a shrinkage estimator for the within-study correlation matrix of binary outcomes in a multivariate generalized linear mixed model framework. By shrinking the empirical correlations toward a parsimonious target (like an exchangeable structure), the method reduces estimation variance, mitigates the 'curse of dimensionality,' and provides a robust foundation for hypothesis testing and pooling of effects across many correlated outcomes.

ideas.title: A Machine Learning Framework to Decompose Funnel Plot Asymmetry into True Small-Study Effects and Rare-Event Artifacts
ideas.description: This research synthesizes machine learning-based pattern recognition with the inversion of traditional asymmetry tests to create a novel analytical framework. The method trains a model on a large corpus of meta-analytic data and simulated datasets where the ground truth (true small-study effect vs. rare-event artifact) is known. By learning the distinct multivariate 'shape signatures' of each cause, the model can analyze a new, observed asymmetric funnel plot and output a decomposed estimate, quantifying the relative probability that the asymmetry stems from genuine bias versus statistical artifact. This provides meta-analysts with a more nuanced, evidence-based interpretation of funnel plot asymmetry to guide subsequent sensitivity analyses or corrections.

ideas.title: Transfer checklist design from aviation to research

ideas.title: Integrate a Robustness Diagnostic with Adaptive Variance Estimation
ideas.description: This idea proposes integrating a modified asymmetry test with robust estimators of between-trial variance by drawing an analogy from geostatistics. In spatial analysis, robust empirical variogram diagnostics are used to inform stable theoretical model fitting. Adapted to meta-analysis, the solution involves a two-stage process: using the modified asymmetry test as a diagnostic to identify studies contributing disproportionately to bias or heterogeneity, and then using this diagnostic output to weight or adjust the calculation of a robust between-trial variance estimator (such as a modified DerSimonian-Laird or Q-profile estimator). This integration creates a more adaptive and resilient meta-analytic framework where the test for asymmetry directly guides and improves the robustness of the critical heterogeneity parameter estimation.

ideas.title: Mapping Bias Direction Using Causal Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that methodological flaws cause bias in a consistent direction (e.g., always toward the null). It draws an analogy from epidemiology and causal inference, where Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) are used to map causal structures and logically deduce the direction of bias introduced by flaws like confounding or selection. The adapted solution is to apply this DAG framework to methodological research. By formally modeling research designs and their potential flaws as causal graphs, researchers can systematically classify conditions under which bias shifts an estimate toward the null, away from the null, or reverses its sign. This moves the field from assumption to principled, graphical prediction of bias direction.

ideas.title: Develop robust funnel asymmetry tests using geophysical signal separation techniques
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from geophysical signal processing to create new statistical tests for funnel asymmetry that perform well under large between-trial heterogeneity. In seismology, techniques like Independent Component Analysis (ICA) are used to separate a target signal from strong, heterogeneous background noise without assuming the noise structure is uniform. By analogically treating the ensemble of study effects in a meta-analysis as a mixed signal, we can develop a test framework that decomposes the observed effects into statistically independent components: the genuine pooled effect, the bias component (funnel asymmetry), and the heterogeneous variance component. This allows for the specific isolation and testing of the asymmetry signal, making the test's performance robust even when the between-trial heterogeneity (τ²) is large, overcoming a key limitation of existing tests like Egger's regression which conflate asymmetry and heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Denoising to Detect Bias Inflection in Cumulative Meta-Analysis
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from geophysics to the original seed idea of using time-series analysis for cumulative meta-analysis. In seismology, the STA/LTA algorithm is a standard method for detecting the onset of seismic events within noisy continuous data by identifying change-points in signal energy. The analogous problem is detecting the point at which the 'signal' of a meta-analytic effect estimate becomes meaningfully influenced by bias. The adapted solution is to treat the sequentially updated pooled estimate from a cumulative meta-analysis as a temporal signal. By calculating a ratio of the variance or deviation of estimates in a short, recent window of studies against a longer baseline window, researchers can algorithmically flag stages in the accumulation of evidence where the trajectory of the summary estimate shifts in a manner suggestive of introducing bias, such as when small-study effects begin to exert influence.

ideas.title: Non-parametric funnel asymmetry test using kernel regression and permutation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astrophysics, where non-parametric kernel regression and permutation testing are used to detect complex, non-Gaussian patterns in Cosmic Microwave Background data. The analogous problem is detecting subtle bias patterns in meta-analysis that violate the linearity assumption of standard funnel plot regression tests. The adapted research idea is to develop a non-linear test for funnel plot asymmetry by using kernel regression to flexibly model the relationship between effect size and precision (or standard error) without assuming a specific functional form. The significance of the estimated non-linear trend, as a measure of asymmetry, is evaluated through a permutation procedure that creates a null distribution under the assumption of no systematic bias, thereby capturing complex, non-linear small-study effects or other bias patterns that linear regression would miss.

ideas.title: Data-Adaptive Asymmetry Tests via Empirical Distribution Learning and Robust Parametric Modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for testing treatment effect asymmetry that does not assume asymptotic normality and actively prevents distributional misspecification. It first uses resampling techniques to non-parametrically estimate the true sampling distribution's characteristics, such as skewness and tail weight. These characteristics then inform the selection and fitting of a flexible parametric model, whose adequacy is rigorously validated. The final test provides valid inference under a wide range of underlying distributions by ensuring the model is data-adaptive and its assumptions are empirically verified.

ideas.title: Network Inconsistency Detection via Electrical Circuit Analogy
ideas.description: This idea extends the modified regression test for bias detection to networks of trials with binary outcomes by drawing an analogy from electrical circuit analysis. The analogous solution uses Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) to detect faults in a network by checking for conservation of flow (current) at each junction. Adapted to network meta-analysis, each treatment becomes a node, and the comparative effect estimates (log odds ratios) between treatments are treated as directed flows. The core method involves applying a modified regression-based test at each treatment node to assess whether the vector of effect estimates entering and leaving that node is internally consistent (i.e., sums to zero, analogous to KCL). A statistically significant violation of this 'conservation law' at a specific node flags localized inconsistency, thereby scaling the bias detection test from a pairwise or global assessment to a node-by-node diagnostic tool that can identify the precise location and contributors to bias within the network geometry.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Signal Processing for Efficient Score Tests with Continuous Meta-Analysis Data
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to extend a modified regression test based on efficient scores to meta-analyses with continuous endpoints. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where matched filtering uses a known signal template to efficiently detect events in continuous seismic data streams, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea involves using the efficient score function from a parametric model for continuous data as a statistical 'template.' By computing its correlation with the aggregated continuous outcome data from multiple studies, we construct a powerful and computationally efficient test for overall effect or between-study heterogeneity. This approach leverages the efficiency properties of scores to handle continuous endpoints, analogous to how matched filtering extracts specific signals from continuous geophysical time series.

ideas.title: Permutation-Based Asymmetry Tests Using Robust Divergence Measures
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology, where comparing complex communities without distributional assumptions is common. The analogous solution uses permutation tests with robust dissimilarity measures. Adapted to causal inference, the research would develop asymmetry tests that replace asymptotic normality with a permutation-based null. A robust divergence measure (e.g., from quantile processes or energy statistics) quantifies the treatment effect distribution asymmetry. The significance is assessed by comparing the observed divergence to an exact permutation distribution, valid for any sampling distribution and finite samples, thus directly challenging the normality assumption.

ideas.title: Apply computer vision techniques to classify funnel plot asymmetry using shape descriptors
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astronomy, where machine learning classifies galaxy shapes based on quantitative morphological features. The analogous problem is identifying asymmetric funnel plots in meta-analysis, which currently relies on subjective visual inspection or statistical tests with limitations. The adapted solution is to treat funnel plots as 2D shapes, extract computer-vision-inspired shape descriptors (e.g., measures of asymmetry, skewness, and density clustering), and use these features to train a classifier. This automates pattern recognition, providing a reproducible, objective tool for detecting publication bias.
